title: Extraction Impacted Tooth
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/02 Dentoalveolar & Implant/Extraction Impacted Tooth.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: Extraction Impacted Tooth

 

Findings: Impacted extraction-impacted-tooth:side:0 mandibular third molar (extraction-impacted-tooth:toothNumber:0) and associated soft tissue lesion (consistent with dental follicle) identified. Tooth accessed via distal hockey stick incision and buccal trough osteotomy. Tooth sectioned and elevated atraumatically. Mental nerve partially visualized and protected. Cystic cavity explored and curetted with no additional pathology noted.

 

The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The patient was escorted to the operating room and placed in supine position on the operating table. Anesthesia monitors were placed. The patient was placed under general anesthesia and intubated via a nasal endotracheal route without incident. The patient was then draped per sterile protocol. The patient was given 2g ancef IV prior to incision.

 

Attention was then directed to the oral cavity. The oral cavity was suctioned free of secretions and debris, and a throat pack was placed. 10 cc of 1% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epi was infiltrated into the extraction-impacted-tooth:side:1 mandibular vestibule and an IAN block was performed.

 

Attention was then drawn to the extraction-impacted-tooth:side:2 mandibular vestibule. Using a #15 blade, a distal hockey stick incision was made in the standard fashion to remove impacted extraction-impacted-tooth:toothNumber:1. This incision was carried forward in a sulcular fashion to extraction-impacted-tooth:toothNumber:2. Using a periosteal elevator, a subperiosteal dissection was completed exposing the mandibular body. The mental nerve was partially visualized but not completely dissected free. An attempt was made to puncture the bone into the lesion using a sharp 18-gauge needle; however, that was not successful due to the thickness of bone. Next, a 702 bur was used to develop a bony window in the lateral portion of the posterior extraction-impacted-tooth:side:3 mandible, and an 18-gauge needle was used to aspirate the lesion, which returned nothing. Next, the 702 bur was used to develop a buccal trough lateral to impacted extraction-impacted-tooth:toothNumber:3 and any overlying bone was also removed in the standard fashion. Next, the 702 bur was used to section extraction-impacted-tooth:toothNumber:4 and both segments were elevated atraumatically. There was an associated soft tissue lesion with extraction-impacted-tooth:toothNumber:5. Clinical appearance was consistent with a dental follicle. Next, the bony window was enlarged to better visualize the cavity and it was connected to the extraction site. A curette was used to explore the cavity and only normal appearing bone was appreciated. There were no cystic or other materials in the cavity and it appeared to be distinct from the third molar site although in close approximation. All sharp bone was then reduced and the flap and all surgical sites were then thoroughly irrigated. Then the incision was closed with 3-0 Vicryl suture taking care to re-suspend papilla.

 

The patient’s oropharynx was then irrigated and suctioned free of debris and throat pack removed. The patient was then awakened and extubated without incidence. The patient was transported to the PACU for recovery and was noted to have stable vital signs and spontaneous respirations on arrival.

title: Facelift + LSR + Lip Filler
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/06 Cosmetic & Facial/Facelift and LSR and Lip Filler.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: Facelift + LSR + Lip Filler

Findings: 

Biplane facelift with inferior extension and platysmaplasty. SMAS flap secured superiorly with 2-0 Vicryl suture. Platysma suspended posterior/superiorly with 2-0 Vicryl suture. Hemostasis with bovie electrocautery and Bipolar electrocautery. Bilateral submandibular gland reduction with Ligasure. Tisseal placed in subcutaneous plane. Closure with staples in the temporal hairline, deep subcutaneous 4-0 Monocryl sutures, and 6-0 Plain gut on the skin. Skin resurfacing with CO2 laser. Voluma lip filler in upper and lower lips.

Technique:

The patient was met in the pre-op holding area where they were identified by two patient identifiers, the patient's NPO status was confirmed, pre-operative HP was completed, all of the patient's questions were answered, the risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the procedure were reviewed and the consent was reviewed and signed, and the patient was marked for surgery. A peripheral IV was placed. The patient was transferred to the operating theater and placed supine on the operating table. The anesthetist induced the patient into general anesthesia and placed an oral endotracheal tube. The surgical team then secured the endotracheal tube. All pressure points were adequately padded and a time out was then performed with all staff members participating in the patient’s care.

Tumescent
Corneal shields were placed with ophthalmic lubrication. The face was prepped with ophthalmic Betadine scrub. Tumescent solution mixture had been prepared by combining 50cc of 2% lidocaine, 1 mg epinephrine (1cc of 1:1,000), and 1 g tranexamic acid (TXA) in 500cc of 0.9% normal saline. This preparation was injected into the subcutaneous plane of the face and neck (~facelift-and-lsr-and-lip-filler:measurement:0 cc). The surgical sites were again prepped with Ophthalmic Betadine scrub and draped in the standard sterile fashion.

Bi-plane Rhytidectomy
#11 used to make submental and bilateral prelobular incisions as entry points for Byron liposuction cannulas. Next, lipodissection was completed on the left, right, and midline of the submental area of the mandible with first a 3mm single hole blunt cannula. The Vaser system was then applied using the 2.5mm 3 ring ultrasonic trocar to liposculpt the left, right and midline of the submental area at facelift-and-lsr-and-lip-filler:measurement:1% on the Vaser ultrasonic device. The time spent with the Vaser was measured to be facelift-and-lsr-and-lip-filler:measurement:2 seconds bilaterally on right and left of midline, and then facelift-and-lsr-and-lip-filler:measurement:3 seconds subcutaneously in midline and facelift-and-lsr-and-lip-filler:measurement:4 seconds subplatysmal in midline. Next, liposuction was then completed with a 2mm single hole blunt cannula periopherally, 3mm single hole blunt cannula, and finally a 4mm single hole blunt cannula on the central aspect of more desired liposuction to the left, right, and midline submental area. This completed the liposuction portion of the procedure. 

Attention was first directed to the left face where a #15 scalpel was used to make a “trilobe” pre-auricular incision with an anterior curvilinear temporal tuft extension. This incision was then continued infra-lobularly then post-auricularly to the height of the intersection of the pinna with the hairline and carried into the hairline with a gentle curve. Electrocautery was used to establish a subcutaneous/supraSMAS dissection plane pre and post-auricularly. Facelift scissors were then used to complete the subcutaneous dissection of the neck region. All access points were then connected pre-to-post auricularly to develop a continuous subcutaneous skin flap with a smooth carpet of fat along the underside. All bleeding was controlled both on the underside of the skin flap as well as on the deep surface with electrocautery. The incision and subcutaneous dissection were then repeated on the right face.

Continuing on the right face a ~3 cm incision through SMAS incision with electrocautery along the trajectory of a line from the angle of the mandible to the lateral canthus of the eye, 1cm inferior to the zygomatic arch and 1cm anterior to the tragus. A sub-SMAS plane was developed to near the anterior border of the parotid. Blunt dissection with vertical spreading was used to extend this plane beyond the anterior border of the parotid until sufficient traction could be applied to the SMAS to achieve the desired lift. The mandibular retaining ligament and masseteric cutaneous ligaments were identified and sharply incised with facelift scissors and a #15 blade. The deep plane was extended to a subplatysmal plane where the submandibular gland was identified. A portion of the lateral aspect of the submandibular gland was excised with a Ligasure. The SMAS was then secured in a superior position with 2-0 vicryl followed by gentle lateral traction and securing 2-0 vicryl sutures. Finally, a running 2-0 vicryl was used to smooth the SMAS surface and correct standing cutaneous deformities. A 2 cm incision was then made vertically through the platysma about 2 cm inferior to the mandibular border followed by a small back cut superiorly. Sub-platysmal dissection was started with electrocautery followed by blunt vertical spreading to further develop the sub-platysmal plane. The submandibular gland was seen to be protuberant and hypertrophic. Ligasure was used for submandibular gland reduction. The platysmal flap was then retracted laterally and superiorly and secured in this position to the post auricular fascia with 2-0 vicryl first at the superior edge then inferiorly and in the middle. A running 2-0 vicryl was again used to smooth standing cutaneous deformities. Loose SMAS/platysma at the mandibular angle was smoothed and secured with running 2-0 vicryl. The SMAS/platysma work was then repeated on the left face.

Continuing on the left face, circumferential undermining was then performed with the electrocautery as indicated to allow for rotation of the skin flap with control and correction of standing cutaneous deformities. All bleeding under the flaps was controlled with electrocautery. Tiseel fibrin sealant was applied to the underside of the flap. The post-auricular skin flaps were then secured with a 0-0 Vicryl suture to hold the flaps in the elevated location. A cutdown was made into excess skin to the height of the postauricular incision where a staple was placed. Excess skin of the flap was removed from the posterior leg of the postauricular incision. Surgical staples were used to secure the flap within the hairline. Subcuticular interrupted 4-0 monocryl sutures were placed to secure the postauricular portion of the flap up to the postauricular sulcus. Excess skin at the temporal tuft and superior lobe of the preauricular incision was excised and secured with a 4-0 monocryl deep dermal stay suture at the posterior-superior extent of the incision. The remaining preauricular excess tissue was excised stepwise at each lobe of the incision and secured with a 4-0 monocryl deep dermal stay sutures at the corner prior to proceeding to the next section. This was carried to the mid earlobe then 5 mm posteriorly and connecting superiorly along the postauricular sulcus to the previous excision. The entire wound edge was secured with 4-0 monocryl deep dermal sutures and an additional layer of running 6-0 plain gut suture was placed epidermally. The skin flap closure was then performed on the right face. Attention was directed to the left earlobe where a beveled incision was made to excise a wedge from the proximal edge and create a curvilinear transition from face to lobule with even and passive approximation of the wound edges. The submental liposuction incision site was closed with a single 5-0 plain gut in interrupted fashion.

CO2 Laser Resurfacing
Laser: Lumenis UltraPulse CO2, 10,600nm
Handpiece: DeepFX
Treatment area: Full face 
Energy: facelift-and-lsr-and-lip-filler:measurement:5 mJ 
Rate: facelift-and-lsr-and-lip-filler:measurement:6 Hz
Density: facelift-and-lsr-and-lip-filler:measurement:7%

Laser: Lumenis UltraPulse CO2, 10,600nm
Handpiece: ActiveFX,
Treatment area: Full face 
Energy: facelift-and-lsr-and-lip-filler:measurement:8 mJ
Rate: facelift-and-lsr-and-lip-filler:measurement:9 Hz
Size: facelift-and-lsr-and-lip-filler:measurement:10 for periorbital area, facelift-and-lsr-and-lip-filler:measurement:11 for remainder of the face
Density: facelift-and-lsr-and-lip-filler:measurement:12%

Lip Filler 

Juvederm Voluma XC was injected into upper and lower lips in even distribution. A total of facelift-and-lsr-and-lip-filler:measurement:13 cc was injected. The sites were noted to be hemostatic. 

Corneal shields were removed and the eyes rinsed with balanced salt solution. The facelift incisions were dressed with bacitracin and Telfa dressing. Next, a headwrap was placed with Bulky Jones and Kerlix. The patient was then turned over to the anesthesia service who awoke the patient, extubated them, and transported them to postop care where they recovered uneventfully.

rawBody:
PROCEDURE: Facelift + LSR + Lip Filler

Findings: 

Biplane facelift with inferior extension and platysmaplasty. SMAS flap secured superiorly with 2-0 Vicryl suture. Platysma suspended posterior/superiorly with 2-0 Vicryl suture. Hemostasis with bovie electrocautery and Bipolar electrocautery. Bilateral submandibular gland reduction with Ligasure. Tisseal placed in subcutaneous plane. Closure with staples in the temporal hairline, deep subcutaneous 4-0 Monocryl sutures, and 6-0 Plain gut on the skin. Skin resurfacing with CO2 laser. Voluma lip filler in upper and lower lips.

Technique:

The patient was met in the pre-op holding area where they were identified by two patient identifiers, the patient's NPO status was confirmed, pre-operative HP was completed, all of the patient's questions were answered, the risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the procedure were reviewed and the consent was reviewed and signed, and the patient was marked for surgery. A peripheral IV was placed. The patient was transferred to the operating theater and placed supine on the operating table. The anesthetist induced the patient into general anesthesia and placed an oral endotracheal tube. The surgical team then secured the endotracheal tube. All pressure points were adequately padded and a time out was then performed with all staff members participating in the patient’s care.

Tumescent
Corneal shields were placed with ophthalmic lubrication. The face was prepped with ophthalmic Betadine scrub. Tumescent solution mixture had been prepared by combining 50cc of 2% lidocaine, 1 mg epinephrine (1cc of 1:1,000), and 1 g tranexamic acid (TXA) in 500cc of 0.9% normal saline. This preparation was injected into the subcutaneous plane of the face and neck (~[X] cc). The surgical sites were again prepped with Ophthalmic Betadine scrub and draped in the standard sterile fashion.

Bi-plane Rhytidectomy
#11 used to make submental and bilateral prelobular incisions as entry points for Byron liposuction cannulas. Next, lipodissection was completed on the left, right, and midline of the submental area of the mandible with first a 3mm single hole blunt cannula. The Vaser system was then applied using the 2.5mm 3 ring ultrasonic trocar to liposculpt the left, right and midline of the submental area at [X]% on the Vaser ultrasonic device. The time spent with the Vaser was measured to be [X] seconds bilaterally on right and left of midline, and then [X] seconds subcutaneously in midline and [X] seconds subplatysmal in midline. Next, liposuction was then completed with a 2mm single hole blunt cannula periopherally, 3mm single hole blunt cannula, and finally a 4mm single hole blunt cannula on the central aspect of more desired liposuction to the left, right, and midline submental area. This completed the liposuction portion of the procedure. 

Attention was first directed to the left face where a #15 scalpel was used to make a “trilobe” pre-auricular incision with an anterior curvilinear temporal tuft extension. This incision was then continued infra-lobularly then post-auricularly to the height of the intersection of the pinna with the hairline and carried into the hairline with a gentle curve. Electrocautery was used to establish a subcutaneous/supraSMAS dissection plane pre and post-auricularly. Facelift scissors were then used to complete the subcutaneous dissection of the neck region. All access points were then connected pre-to-post auricularly to develop a continuous subcutaneous skin flap with a smooth carpet of fat along the underside. All bleeding was controlled both on the underside of the skin flap as well as on the deep surface with electrocautery. The incision and subcutaneous dissection were then repeated on the right face.

Continuing on the right face a ~3 cm incision through SMAS incision with electrocautery along the trajectory of a line from the angle of the mandible to the lateral canthus of the eye, 1cm inferior to the zygomatic arch and 1cm anterior to the tragus. A sub-SMAS plane was developed to near the anterior border of the parotid. Blunt dissection with vertical spreading was used to extend this plane beyond the anterior border of the parotid until sufficient traction could be applied to the SMAS to achieve the desired lift. The mandibular retaining ligament and masseteric cutaneous ligaments were identified and sharply incised with facelift scissors and a #15 blade. The deep plane was extended to a subplatysmal plane where the submandibular gland was identified. A portion of the lateral aspect of the submandibular gland was excised with a Ligasure. The SMAS was then secured in a superior position with 2-0 vicryl followed by gentle lateral traction and securing 2-0 vicryl sutures. Finally, a running 2-0 vicryl was used to smooth the SMAS surface and correct standing cutaneous deformities. A 2 cm incision was then made vertically through the platysma about 2 cm inferior to the mandibular border followed by a small back cut superiorly. Sub-platysmal dissection was started with electrocautery followed by blunt vertical spreading to further develop the sub-platysmal plane. The submandibular gland was seen to be protuberant and hypertrophic. Ligasure was used for submandibular gland reduction. The platysmal flap was then retracted laterally and superiorly and secured in this position to the post auricular fascia with 2-0 vicryl first at the superior edge then inferiorly and in the middle. A running 2-0 vicryl was again used to smooth standing cutaneous deformities. Loose SMAS/platysma at the mandibular angle was smoothed and secured with running 2-0 vicryl. The SMAS/platysma work was then repeated on the left face.

Continuing on the left face, circumferential undermining was then performed with the electrocautery as indicated to allow for rotation of the skin flap with control and correction of standing cutaneous deformities. All bleeding under the flaps was controlled with electrocautery. Tiseel fibrin sealant was applied to the underside of the flap. The post-auricular skin flaps were then secured with a 0-0 Vicryl suture to hold the flaps in the elevated location. A cutdown was made into excess skin to the height of the postauricular incision where a staple was placed. Excess skin of the flap was removed from the posterior leg of the postauricular incision. Surgical staples were used to secure the flap within the hairline. Subcuticular interrupted 4-0 monocryl sutures were placed to secure the postauricular portion of the flap up to the postauricular sulcus. Excess skin at the temporal tuft and superior lobe of the preauricular incision was excised and secured with a 4-0 monocryl deep dermal stay suture at the posterior-superior extent of the incision. The remaining preauricular excess tissue was excised stepwise at each lobe of the incision and secured with a 4-0 monocryl deep dermal stay sutures at the corner prior to proceeding to the next section. This was carried to the mid earlobe then 5 mm posteriorly and connecting superiorly along the postauricular sulcus to the previous excision. The entire wound edge was secured with 4-0 monocryl deep dermal sutures and an additional layer of running 6-0 plain gut suture was placed epidermally. The skin flap closure was then performed on the right face. Attention was directed to the left earlobe where a beveled incision was made to excise a wedge from the proximal edge and create a curvilinear transition from face to lobule with even and passive approximation of the wound edges. The submental liposuction incision site was closed with a single 5-0 plain gut in interrupted fashion.

CO2 Laser Resurfacing
Laser: Lumenis UltraPulse CO2, 10,600nm
Handpiece: DeepFX
Treatment area: Full face 
Energy: [X] mJ 
Rate: [X] Hz
Density: [X]%

Laser: Lumenis UltraPulse CO2, 10,600nm
Handpiece: ActiveFX,
Treatment area: Full face 
Energy: [X] mJ
Rate: [X] Hz
Size: [X] for periorbital area, [X] for remainder of the face
Density: [X]%

Lip Filler 

Juvederm Voluma XC was injected into upper and lower lips in even distribution. A total of [X] cc was injected. The sites were noted to be hemostatic. 

Corneal shields were removed and the eyes rinsed with balanced salt solution. The facelift incisions were dressed with bacitracin and Telfa dressing. Next, a headwrap was placed with Bulky Jones and Kerlix. The patient was then turned over to the anesthesia service who awoke the patient, extubated them, and transported them to postop care where they recovered uneventfully.

[Attending] was present for the entirety of the case.

title: Hardware Removal (Le Fort)
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/01 Trauma/Hardware Removal (Le Fort).md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: Hardware Removal (Le Fort)

Findings: 

Maxillary circum-vestibular incision from 1st molar to 1st molar, hardware exposed after removing fibrous tissue, round diamond bur used to reveal screw surface, osteotome used to elevate the hardware, all screws removed, site copiously irrigated with saline, site closed primarily with 3-0 Vicryl suture in simple and interrupted fashion.

Technique:

The patient was met in the preoperative holding area. The plan for the surgery was reviewed with the patient. All patient questions were answered. The history and physical examination was updated. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered.

The patient was then transported to the operating room and placed in the supine position on the operating room table. General anesthesia was then induced with the anesthesia team via the intravenous route, and the airway was secured with a nasoendotracheal tube. Pressure points were padded and checked. At this time, the oropharynx was suctioned free of debris. The patient was prepped and draped in sterile fashion. A surgical time-out was performed. The maxillary surgical sites were anesthetized with lidocaine 2% with 1:100,000 epinephrine with local infiltration.

Attention was then directed to the patient's maxillary buccal vestibule where a circumvestibular incision was initiated using bovie electrocautery starting at the mucosa of the left first molar approximately 5 mm superior to the mucogingival junction to the area of the right first molar taking care to follow prior incision line. This incision was then carried down to bone. Periosteal elevators were used to reflect a full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap to expose the single-piece maxillary LeFort hardware that extended from the right maxillary buttress to the left maxillary buttress. The flap was also extended superiorly to expose the right and left piriform rims and the right and left infraorbital neurovascular bundles. Both right and left neurovascular bundles were noted to be intact and continued to remain so for the duration of the case.

With subperiosteal dissection complete, it was noted that the most inferior and posterior screw on the left maxilla was encased within soft tissue and noted to have been reflected within the soft tissue. A small stab incision with a #15 blade was used to expose screw from soft tissue and screw retrieved using hemostats. A second screw was noted to have been released from the hardware through soft tissue dissection at the piriform rim and again retrieved with a curved hemostat. It was noted that in the right maxillary malar region, purulence was encountered and expressed near site of loose screw. This site was thoroughly irrigated with sterile saline and care was taken to completely express purulence from the site. The hardware was noted to be well adapted and partially osseointegrated to the bone with significant bony coverage at the piriform rims bilaterally and the junction of the osteotomy bilaterally. A #2 round diamond bur under copious irrigation along with various osteotomes were used to completely expose the hardware. All screws were removed and accounted for using a universal driver. A mallet was used to ensure seating of the driver to facilitate removal. A single screw head on the left dentate segment was sheared off with attempted removal. Once hardware was fully removed, screw threads from sheared screw head on left maxilla was removed with a needle driver. The surgical site was again thoroughly irrigated with copious amounts of sterile saline. 

Attention was then directed toward closure of the maxilla. Passive drape of the nasal septum was verified with no buckling. The entire surgical site was irrigated. The alar base was reduced with 2-0 PDS suture with an alar cinch. Midline was tacked with a mattress suture with a 3-0 Vicryl suture. The circumvestibular incision of the maxilla was closed with a running 3-0 Vicryl suture.

At this time, the oropharynx was irrigated and suctioned free of debris. An orogastric tube was passed, decompressing the stomach. Occlusion was then rechecked and confirmed appropriately. 10cc of exparel was injected at maxillary surgical site via local infiltration. The face was cleaned with moist ray-tecs. The head wrap was removed and the patient was turned over to the anesthesia team. The lips were liberally lubricated with hydrocortisone cream. The patient was extubated in the main operating room and transferred to the PACU with appropriate vital signs and spontaneous respirations, having tolerated the procedures well.

hardware-removal-le-fort:text:0 was present and scrubbed for the duration of the surgery

title: Incision and Drainage of Submandibular Abscess
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/03 Pathology, Salivary & Infection/Incision and Drainage of Submandibular Abscess.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: Incision and Drainage of Submandibular Abscess

 

Findings: incision-and-drainage-of-submandibular-abscess:side:0 submandibular abscess fluid collection identified on the medial surface of the posterior mandible. Tooth incision-and-drainage-of-submandibular-abscess:toothNumber:0 extracted atraumatically. Submandibular space accessed via extraoral incision. Scant drainage observed and sent for Gram stain and aerobic/anaerobic culture and sensitivity. A 0.25 inch Penrose drain was placed and secured.

 

The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The patient was escorted to the operating room and placed in supine position on the operating table. Anesthesia monitors were placed. The patient was placed under general anesthesia and intubated with an oral endotracheal tube without incident. The patient was then draped per sterile protocol.

 

Attention was then directed to the oral cavity. The oral cavity was suctioned free of secretions and debris and throat pack was placed. 5 cc of 1% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epi was infiltrated into the mandibular vestibules approximating incision-and-drainage-of-submandibular-abscess:toothNumber:1 in addition to performing an inferior alveolar nerve block on that side. Next tooth incision-and-drainage-of-submandibular-abscess:toothNumber:2 was extracted atraumatically with a dental elevator and forceps. There was no damage to adjacent structures. The extraction site was curetted and thoroughly irrigated with sterile saline.

 

Next attention was then drawn to the incision-and-drainage-of-submandibular-abscess:side:1 submandibular region. An incision was planned greater than incision-and-drainage-of-submandibular-abscess:measurement:0 cm from the inferior border of the mandible and 4cc of 1% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine was injected in a subdermal plane. Next a incision-and-drainage-of-submandibular-abscess:measurement:1 cm incision was made using a #15 blade at the planned position. The incision was made through the dermis, subcutaneous fat, platysma and underlying fascia. Blunt dissection was performed with a curved hemostat  in a closed position to the inferior border of the mandible. The hemostat was used to explore the area of observed fluid collection- on the medial surface of the posterior mandible. There was scant drainage observed which was sampled on a sterile cotton tipped applicator and sent for Gram stain and aerobic/anaerobic culture and sensitivity. The site was thoroughly irrigated with sterile saline and a 0.25 inch penrose drain was placed into the incision and then secured with a 2-0 nylon suture to the skin. A sterile 4x4 dressing was placed over the drain and the site was hemostatic with minimal drainage.

 

The patient’s oropharynx was then irrigated and suctioned free of debris and throat pack removed. The patient was then awakened and extubated without incidence. The patient was transported to the PACU for recovery and was noted to have stable vital signs and spontaneous respirations on arrival.

title: Mandibular Tori Removal Bilateral
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/02 Dentoalveolar & Implant/Mandibular Tori Removal Bilateral.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: Mandibular Tori Removal Bilateral

Findings: Bilateral mandibular tori present. Accessed via bilateral lingual sulcular incisions. Tori successfully removed utilizing trough osteotomies and T-handle osteotomes. Sharp bone smoothed. Floor of the mouth and Wharton's duct remained intact bilaterally.

 

The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The patient was escorted to the operating room and placed in supine position on the operating table. Anesthesia monitors were placed. The patient was placed under general anesthesia and intubated via a nasal endotracheal route without incident. The patient was then draped per sterile protocol. The patient was given 2g ancef IV prior to incision.

 

Attention was then directed to the oral cavity. The oral cavity was suctioned free of secretions and debris, and a throat pack was placed. 10 cc of 1% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epi was infiltrated into the right and left lingual mucosa achieving hydrodissection over the tori and bilateral IAN blocks were performed. 

Attention was directed to the right mandible. A lingual sulcular incision was made with a 15 blade from first molar to midline. A full thickness mucoperiosteal lingual flap was raised. A lingual trough osteotomy was initiated with a 701 bur under copious irrigation,  mandibular-tori-removal-bilateral:measurement:0 mm from the adjacent teeth, establishing a cleavage plane for torus removal. With a malleable retractor protecting the floor of the mouth, a T-handle osteotome were then used to complete the osteotomy and remove the torus. Sharp corners of bone were smoothed with a hand rasp. The site was irrigated with sterile saline thoroughly.

 

Attention was directed to the left mandible, where the same steps were carried out. The torus was removed without complications. Floor of the mouth and Wharton's duct intact bilaterally. The site was irrigated with sterile saline thoroughly. Hemostasis ensured. The bilateral mandibular sites were closed primarily with interrupted 3-0 chromic gut sutures.

The throat was suctioned and all debris was cleared. An orogastric tube was passed successfully by the surgical team. The face was cleaned with moist ray-tec gauze.  The patient was turned over to the care of the anesthesia team, and was extubated in the main operating room and transferred to the PACU with stable vital signs and spontaneous respirations.

The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The patient was escorted to the operating room and placed in supine position on the operating table. Anesthesia monitors were placed. The patient was placed under general anesthesia and intubated via a nasal endotracheal route without incident. The patient was then draped per sterile protocol. The patient was given 2g ancef IV prior to incision.

 

Attention was then directed to the oral cavity. The oral cavity was suctioned free of secretions and debris, and a throat pack was placed. 10 cc of 1% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epi was infiltrated into the right and left lingual mucosa achieving hydrodissection over the tori and bilateral IAN blocks were performed. 

Attention was directed to the right mandible. A lingual sulcular incision was made with a 15 blade from first molar to midline. A full thickness mucoperiosteal lingual flap was raised. A lingual trough osteotomy was initiated with a 701 bur under copious irrigation,  [X] mm from the adjacent teeth, establishing a cleavage plane for torus removal. With a malleable retractor protecting the floor of the mouth, a T-handle osteotome were then used to complete the osteotomy and remove the torus. Sharp corners of bone were smoothed with a hand rasp. The site was irrigated with sterile saline thoroughly.

 

Attention was directed to the left mandible, where the same steps were carried out. The torus was removed without complications. Floor of the mouth and Wharton's duct intact bilaterally. The site was irrigated with sterile saline thoroughly. Hemostasis ensured. The bilateral mandibular sites were closed primarily with interrupted 3-0 chromic gut sutures.

The throat was suctioned and all debris was cleared. An orogastric tube was passed successfully by the surgical team. The face was cleaned with moist ray-tec gauze.  The patient was turned over to the care of the anesthesia team, and was extubated in the main operating room and transferred to the PACU with stable vital signs and spontaneous respirations.  

[Attending] was present for the entirety of the case.

title: Minituck, Blepharoplasty Lowers, LSR
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/06 Cosmetic & Facial/Minituck, Blepharoplasty Lowers, LSR.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: Minituck, Blepharoplasty Lowers, LSR

Findings: 



Submental liposuction/liposculpting (Byron cannulas + Vaser) of left, right, and midline submental fat
Bilateral lower blepharoplasty via transconjunctival incision with orbital fat repositioning (lateral/central pads transposed over the arcus marginalis); no fat or skin excised; Doyle-splint bolster placed
Short-scar (mini) biplane rhytidectomy: subcutaneous/supra-SMAS flap, SMAS plication/imbrication with 2-0 Vicryl; partial paramedian platysma resection with anterior corset platysmaplasty
Tisseel to flap undersurface; closure with subcuticular 4-0 Monocryl + running 6-0 plain gut; earlobe wedge as needed
Full-face CO2 ablative laser skin resurfacing: DeepFX (25 mJ, 600 Hz, 5% density) and ActiveFX (80 mJ periorbital / 100 mJ remainder, 150 Hz, 3% density)

Technique: 

The patient arrived at Walter Reed and was met in the pre-op holding area where they were identified by two patient identifiers, the patient's NPO status was confirmed, pre-operative HP was completed, all of the patient's questions were answered, the risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the procedure were reviewed and the consent was reviewed and signed, and the patient was marked for surgery. A peripheral IV was placed. The patient was transferred to the operating theater and placed supine on the operating table. The anesthetist induced the patient into general anesthesia and placed an oral endotracheal tube. The surgical team then secured the endotracheal tube. All pressure points were adequately padded and a time out was then performed with all staff members participating in the patient’s care.

Corneal shields were placed with ophthalmic lubrication. The face was prepped with ophthalmic Betadine scrub. Tumescent solution mixture had been prepared by combining 50cc of 2% lidocaine, 1 mg epinephrine (1cc of 1:1,000), and 1 g tranexamic acid (TXA) in 500cc of 0.9% normal saline. This preparation was injected into the subcutaneous plane of the face and neck (~minituck-blepharoplasty-lowers-lsr:measurement:0 cc). The surgical sites were again prepped with Ophthalmic Betadine scrub and draped in the standard sterile fashion.

Bilateral lower blepharoplasties/fat pad repositioning
A Colorado Bovie tip was used to make a transconjunctival incision and expose the inferior orbital fat pads bilaterally. The inferior orbital rim arcus marginalis was released with bovie and periosteal elevators and subperiosteal dissection was carried inferior to the orbital rim. The lateral and central orbital fat pads were identified and mobilized. Bleeding was encountered on both sides and controlled with electrocautery. Infraorbital nerves were visualized bilaterally, with bipolar cautery used in proximity to L V2. Using 4-0 monocryl suture, the fat was repositioned by passing the suture from the lateral and central fat pads medially to exit through the skin of the melolabial fold near the ala. The suture was then passed back into the skin near the ala of the melolabial fold, creating a loop. A bolster dressing fashioned out of half a Doyle splint was placed under this loop of suture to provide additional support and prevent skin dimpling. The suture was then passed back laterally into the central and lateral fat pads and tied, securing the repositioned fat in its new position to address the tear trough deformity and provide midface volume restoration. This technique was repeated bilaterally with careful attention to symmetry. Hemostasis was confirmed. No excess orbital fat was excised and no skin excision was performed given the transconjunctival approach.

Bi-plane Rhytidectomy:
#11 used to make submental and bilateral prelobular incisions as entry points for Byron liposuction cannulas. Next, lipodissection was completed on the left, right, and midline of the submental area of the mandible with a 3mm single hole blunt cannula. The Vaser system was then applied using the 2.5mm 3 ring ultrasonic trocar to liposculpt the left, right and midline of the submental area at minituck-blepharoplasty-lowers-lsr:measurement:1% on the Vaser ultrasonic device. Next, liposuction was then completed with a 3mm single hole blunt cannula to the left, right, and midline submental area. This completed the liposuction portion of the procedure. Hemostasis was achieved with bovie and bipolar. Incision closed with 4-0 monocryl in subcuticular fashion.

Attention was first directed to the left face where a #15 scalpel was used to make a “trilobe” pre-auricular incision with an anterior curvilinear temporal tuft extension. This incision was then continued infra-lobularly then ~4cm posteriorly in the postauricular region with a 2cm perpendicular cutback. Electrocautery was used to establish a subcutaneous/supraSMAS dissection plane pre and post-auricularly. Byron cannulas were used once again to help establish and define subcutaneous plane. Facelift scissors were then used to complete the subcutaneous dissection of the neck region. All access points were then connected pre-to-post auricularly to develop a continuous subcutaneous skin flap with a smooth carpet of fat along the underside. A 2cm elliptical incision was made around submental lipodissection port, supra-platysmal dissection was performed using facelift scissors and Bovie electrocautery to elevate the overlying skin and subcutaneous tissues. Partial platysma resection was completed with excision of several segments of platysma in the paramedian submental areas measuring ~1cm from the inferior border of the mandible. Anterior corset suturing was performed to medialize the lateral plastyma segments. All bleeding was controlled both on the underside of the skin flap as well as on the deep surface with electrocautery.

Continuing on the right face a ~3 cm incision through SMAS incision with electrocautery along the trajectory of a line from the angle of the mandible to the lateral canthus of the eye, 1cm inferior to the zygomatic arch and 1cm anterior to the tragus. A sub-SMAS plane was developed to near the anterior border of the parotid. Blunt dissection with vertical spreading was used to extend this plane beyond the anterior border of the parotid until sufficient traction could be applied to the SMAS to achieve the desired lift. The SMAS was then secured in a superior position with 2-0 vicryl followed by gentle lateral traction and securing 2-0 vicryl sutures. Finally, a running 2-0 vicryl was used to smooth the SMAS surface and correct standing cutaneous deformities. A 2 cm incision was then made vertically through the platysma about 2 cm inferior to the mandibular border followed by a small back cut superiorly. Sub-platysmal dissection was started with electrocautery followed by blunt vertical spreading to further develop the sub-platysmal plane. The submandibular gland was seen to be protuberant and hypertrophic. The platysmal flap was then retracted laterally and superiorly and secured in this position to the post auricular fascia with 2-0 vicryl first at the superior edge then inferiorly and in the middle. A running 2-0 vicryl was again used to smooth standing cutaneous deformities. Loose SMAS/platysma at the mandibular angle was smoothed and secured with running 2-0 vicryl. The SMAS/platysma work was then repeated on the left face.

Continuing on the left face, circumferential undermining was then performed with the electrocautery as indicated to allow for rotation of the skin flap with control and correction of standing cutaneous deformities. All bleeding under the flaps was controlled with electrocautery. Tisseel fibrin sealant was applied to the underside of the flap. The post-auricular skin flaps were then secured with a 0-0 Vicryl suture to hold the flaps in the elevated location. A cutdown was made into excess skin to the height of the postauricular incision. Excess skin of the flap was removed from the posterior leg of the postauricular incision. Subcuticular interrupted 4-0 monocryl sutures were placed to secure the postauricular portion of the flap up to the postauricular sulcus. Excess skin at the temporal tuft and superior lobe of the preauricular incision was excised and secured with a 4-0 monocryl deep dermal stay suture at the posterior-superior extent of the incision.

The incision was closed in layers with 4-0 Vicryl suture for the deeper tissues and appropriate closure of the skin layer. The remaining preauricular excess tissue was excised stepwise at each lobe of the incision and secured with a 4-0 monocryl deep dermal stay sutures at the corner prior to proceeding to the next section. This was carried to the mid earlobe then 5 mm posteriorly and connecting superiorly along the postauricular sulcus to the previous excision. The entire wound edge was secured with 4-0 monocryl deep dermal sutures and an additional layer of running 6-0 plain gut suture was placed epidermally. The skin flap closure was then performed on the right face. Attention was directed to the earlobes where a beveled incision was made to excise a wedge from the proximal edge and create a curvilinear transition from face to lobule with even and passive approximation of the wound edges. The incision was then closed with running 5-0 plain gut.

CO2 Laser Resurfacing
Laser: Lumenis UltraPulse CO2, 10,600nm
Handpiece: DeepFX
Treatment area: Full face 
Energy: minituck-blepharoplasty-lowers-lsr:measurement:2 mJ for full-face
Density minituck-blepharoplasty-lowers-lsr:measurement:3%
Rate: minituck-blepharoplasty-lowers-lsr:measurement:4 Hz

Laser: Lumenis UltraPulse CO2, 10,600nm
Handpiece: ActiveFX
Treatment area: Full face 
Energy: minituck-blepharoplasty-lowers-lsr:measurement:5 mJ for peri-orbital area, minituck-blepharoplasty-lowers-lsr:measurement:6 mJ at the remainder of the face
Density minituck-blepharoplasty-lowers-lsr:measurement:7%
Rate: minituck-blepharoplasty-lowers-lsr:measurement:8 Hz

External eye shields were worn by the patient during the entire procedure, and wavelength specific eye protection was worn by all staff in the room. Mirrors were stowed, and opaque film applied to the windows. The vacuum was used for smoke evacuation, and laser masks were worn by all staff in the room. Fire extinguishers were on standby, and a wet gauze/towel was on the field. Pulses were delivered in rows with minimal overlap. Aquaphor was applied to the entire treated area at the conclusion of the procedure.
Corneal shields were removed and the eyes rinsed with balanced salt solution. The facelift incisions were dressed with bacitracin and Telfa dressing. Next, a headwrap was placed with Bulky Jones and Kerlix. The patient was then turned over to the anesthesia service who awoke the patient, extubated them, and transported them to postop care where they recovered uneventfully.

minituck-blepharoplasty-lowers-lsr:text:0 was present for the entirety of the case.

title: Nasal Bone Reduction
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/01 Trauma/Nasal Bone Reduction.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: Nasal Bone Reduction

 

Findings: Displaced nasal bone fracture and septum identified. Closed reduction performed using Walsham forceps and Boies elevator. Midline reduction with normal anatomic contours confirmed. Doyle splints placed and secured bilaterally. nasal-bone-reduction:side:0 nare packed with iodoform gauze for support. External Denver splint placed.

 

The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient and escort. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The patient was escorted to the operating room and placed in supine position on the operating table. Anesthesia monitors were placed. The patient was placed under general anesthesia and intubated via an oral endotracheal route without incident. The patient was then draped per sterile protocol.

 

Attention was then directed to the nasal cavity and nose. Afrin soaked pledgets were placed in both nostrils while local anesthesia was administered. Bilateral infraorbital and supratrochlear  blocks were performed with 10cc of 1% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine. Additionally, local anesthetic was delivered to the area overlying the nasal bones and the septum. The pledgets were then removed from the nose.

 

Walsham forceps were then used to reduce the nasal septum and the nasal bones. A Boies elevator was also used. The nasal bone fracture and septum were noted to be midline with normal anatomic contours. With proper anatomical reduction confirmed, Doyle splints were then placed in bilateral nares. A 2-0 silk tie was used to secure the Doyle splints to the septum. 1/4 inch iodoform packing was placed in the nasal-bone-reduction:side:1 nare under the fractured nasal bones to provide support and then sutured to the nostril with 2-0 silk. An external Denver splint was then placed overtop of Steri-Strips.

 

This concludes the surgical procedure. Care of the patient was then turned over to the anesthesia team. The patient was extubated in the operating room without incident. The patient was transferred from the operating room to the recovery room with stable vital signs and spontaneous respirations verified.

title: ORIF Mandible with Recon bar
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/01 Trauma/ORIF Mandible with Recon bar.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: ORIF Mandible with Recon bar

 



Findings: orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:side:0 mandibular alveolar/body, orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:side:1 mandibular, and posterior orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:side:2 mandibular fractures identified. orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:side:3 body fracture accessed via vestibular incision; mental nerve visualized and intact. Fractures placed in maxillomandibular fixation (MMF). orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:side:4 mandibular fracture reduced and fixated with a orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:hardwareCount:0-hole straight plate and bicortical screws. Posterior orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:side:5 mandibular fracture reduced and fixated with a orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:hardwareCount:1-hole straight plate and bicortical screws. orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:side:6 alveolar fracture reduced with a bridle wire. Occlusion verified as stable and native.

 

The patient was brought to the pre-operative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The anesthesia team saw and evaluated the patient and obtained written informed consent for general anesthesia. The patient elected to proceed as planned.

 

Next, the patient was transported to the operating room and transferred to the operating table. They were positioned for comfort and pressure points were padded. General anesthesia was induced via nasoendotracheal tube which was subsequently secured with tape. The patient's face was prepped with betadine and oral cavity prepped with peridex rinse. They were then draped in standard fashion with surgical towels and drapes.

 

Once in a stable plane of anesthesia and when all team members were present, a standard time-out procedure was done to verify patient name, age, date of birth, diagnosis, ASA Classification, procedure, site, side, risk of blood loss, fire score, allergies, and anesthetic type. All were in accord and the decision was made to begin the procedure.

 

10 ml 2% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine was injected to the mandibular surgical sites.

 

Next a total of orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:hardwareCount:2 orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:measurement:0-mm intermaxillary fixation (IMF) screws were placed into the mandible and maxilla with even distribution (orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:hardwareCount:3 in the maxilla, orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:hardwareCount:4 in the mandible). Attention was directed towards the orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:side:7 mandibular alveolar and body fractures. A vestibular incision, 5 mm below the mucogingival junction, was made from tooth orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:toothNumber:0 and carried down to bone with electrocautery, staying superior the mental foramen. A subperiosteal dissection performed with a #9 periosteal elevator. The mental foramen and mental nerve was visualized, isolated, skeletonized and remained intact throughout the procedure. The fracture site was well exposed including the inferior border of the mandible in this region. The fracture was mobilized at this time. The sites were thoroughly irrigated with sterile saline.

 

Next with all fractures of the mandible exposed and evaluated for reduction, the patient was placed tightly into maxillomandibular fixation (MMF) with 24-gauge wires spanning between IMF screws in a looped fashion. Occlusion was thoroughly evaluated and verified to be native and stable in appearance.

 

Next attention was turned to the orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:side:8 mandibular fracture. The fracture reduction was evaluated and noted to be satisfactory. Next a orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:text:0 profile, orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:hardwareCount:5-hole straight plate was adapted equally across the fracture site on the inferior border. It was secured with orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:hardwareCount:6 bicortical screws. The fracture was re-evaluated and noted to be well reduced and stable. The occlusion was also re-evaluated and noted to still be stable and native in appearance. This site was then thoroughly irrigated with sterile saline.

 

Next attention was turned to the posterior orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:side:9 mandible fracture. The fracture reduction was evaluated and noted to be satisfactory. Next, a orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:text:1 profile, orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:hardwareCount:7-hole straight plate was adapted equally across the fracture site on the inferior border of the mandible and well secured with orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:hardwareCount:8 orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:text:2 screws in a bicortical fashion. The fracture was re-evaluated and noted to be well reduced and stable. The occlusion was also re-evaluated and noted to still be stable and native in appearance. This site was then thoroughly irrigated with sterile saline.

 

Next the MMF was released and occlusion and fracture sites evaluated. The occlusion and reduction were re-evaluated and noted to be stable, native, anatomic and reproducible. Next after having thoroughly inspected all surgical sites, they were then once again irrigated with a copious amount of sterile saline. All sites were noted to be hemostatic.

 

A bridle wire was placed between teeth orif-mandible-with-recon-bar:toothNumber:1 to appropriately reduce the alveolar fracture.

 

Next the mentalis was re-suspended with 3-0 vicryl sutures utilized in a running fashion which was reinforced with several simple interrupted 3-0 vicryl sutures. The mucosa was re-approximated with 3-0 chromic gut in a simple running fashion.

 

The patient was then placed in guiding elastics.

 

This concluded the surgical procedure and the care of the patient was turned over to the anesthesia team. The patient was extubated in the main operating room and transferred to the PACU in stable condition. When criteria were met, the patient was transferred to the inpatient ward for continued management until discharge.

 

The procedure was uncomplicated and the patient remained hemodynamically stable throughout, tolerating both the procedure and anesthesia well.

PROCEDURE: ORIF Mandible with Recon bar

 

[TITLE CHECK — 2026-06-14: Title says "with Recon bar," but the technique uses load-sharing straight plates (6-hole + 4-hole) and a bridle wire — no reconstruction bar is placed. Either retitle (e.g., "ORIF Mandible, Multiple Fractures") or add the recon-bar step if one was used.]

Findings: [R/L] mandibular alveolar/body, [R/L] mandibular, and posterior [R/L] mandibular fractures identified. [R/L] body fracture accessed via vestibular incision; mental nerve visualized and intact. Fractures placed in maxillomandibular fixation (MMF). [R/L] mandibular fracture reduced and fixated with a [#]-hole straight plate and bicortical screws. Posterior [R/L] mandibular fracture reduced and fixated with a [#]-hole straight plate and bicortical screws. [R/L] alveolar fracture reduced with a bridle wire. Occlusion verified as stable and native.

 

The patient was brought to the pre-operative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The anesthesia team saw and evaluated the patient and obtained written informed consent for general anesthesia. The patient elected to proceed as planned.

 

Next, the patient was transported to the operating room and transferred to the operating table. They were positioned for comfort and pressure points were padded. General anesthesia was induced via nasoendotracheal tube which was subsequently secured with tape. The patient's face was prepped with betadine and oral cavity prepped with peridex rinse. They were then draped in standard fashion with surgical towels and drapes.

 

Once in a stable plane of anesthesia and when all team members were present, a standard time-out procedure was done to verify patient name, age, date of birth, diagnosis, ASA Classification, procedure, site, side, risk of blood loss, fire score, allergies, and anesthetic type. All were in accord and the decision was made to begin the procedure.

 

10 ml 2% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine was injected to the mandibular surgical sites.

 

Next a total of [#] [length]-mm intermaxillary fixation (IMF) screws were placed into the mandible and maxilla with even distribution ([#] in the maxilla, [#] in the mandible). Attention was directed towards the [R/L] mandibular alveolar and body fractures. A vestibular incision, 5 mm below the mucogingival junction, was made from tooth [#__] and carried down to bone with electrocautery, staying superior the mental foramen. A subperiosteal dissection performed with a #9 periosteal elevator. The mental foramen and mental nerve was visualized, isolated, skeletonized and remained intact throughout the procedure. The fracture site was well exposed including the inferior border of the mandible in this region. The fracture was mobilized at this time. The sites were thoroughly irrigated with sterile saline.

 

Next with all fractures of the mandible exposed and evaluated for reduction, the patient was placed tightly into maxillomandibular fixation (MMF) with 24-gauge wires spanning between IMF screws in a looped fashion. Occlusion was thoroughly evaluated and verified to be native and stable in appearance.

 

Next attention was turned to the [R/L] mandibular fracture. The fracture reduction was evaluated and noted to be satisfactory. Next a [profile] profile, [#]-hole straight plate was adapted equally across the fracture site on the inferior border. It was secured with [#] bicortical screws. The fracture was re-evaluated and noted to be well reduced and stable. The occlusion was also re-evaluated and noted to still be stable and native in appearance. This site was then thoroughly irrigated with sterile saline.

 

Next attention was turned to the posterior [R/L] mandible fracture. The fracture reduction was evaluated and noted to be satisfactory. Next, a [profile] profile, [#]-hole straight plate was adapted equally across the fracture site on the inferior border of the mandible and well secured with [#] [profile] screws in a bicortical fashion. The fracture was re-evaluated and noted to be well reduced and stable. The occlusion was also re-evaluated and noted to still be stable and native in appearance. This site was then thoroughly irrigated with sterile saline.

 

Next the MMF was released and occlusion and fracture sites evaluated. The occlusion and reduction were re-evaluated and noted to be stable, native, anatomic and reproducible. Next after having thoroughly inspected all surgical sites, they were then once again irrigated with a copious amount of sterile saline. All sites were noted to be hemostatic.

 

A bridle wire was placed between teeth [#__] to appropriately reduce the alveolar fracture.

 

Next the mentalis was re-suspended with 3-0 vicryl sutures utilized in a running fashion which was reinforced with several simple interrupted 3-0 vicryl sutures. The mucosa was re-approximated with 3-0 chromic gut in a simple running fashion.

 

The patient was then placed in guiding elastics.

 

This concluded the surgical procedure and the care of the patient was turned over to the anesthesia team. The patient was extubated in the main operating room and transferred to the PACU in stable condition. When criteria were met, the patient was transferred to the inpatient ward for continued management until discharge.

 

The procedure was uncomplicated and the patient remained hemodynamically stable throughout, tolerating both the procedure and anesthesia well.

title: ORIF NOE, Le Fort, ZMC, Orbit
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/01 Trauma/ORIF NOE, Le Fort, ZMC, Orbit.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: ORIF NOE, Le Fort, ZMC, Orbit

 

Findings: orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:0 ZMC fracture mobile and displaced. ZF accessed via lateral brow approach, fixated with KLS orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:text:0 plate and orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:0 monocortical screws. orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:1 infraorbital rim exposed via transconjunctival approach, fixated with KLS orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:text:1 plate and orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:1 monocortical screws. Bilateral Le Fort II fractures exposed via Keen incision. ZM and piriform rims fixated with orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:text:2 KLS L-plates and monocortical screws. Bilateral NOE fractures accessed via orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:2 transconjunctival approach and two connecting Lynch incisions (H-incision), noted to be severely comminuted and depressed. NOE fractures fixated with multiple KLS orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:text:3 plates and monocortical screws. orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:3 orbital floor reconstruction completed with KLS Resorb-X plate. Bilateral forced duction tests completed with EOMI. Bilateral IO nerves visualized and noted to be intact throughout.

Technique:

The patient was brought to the pre-operative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient's family. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The anesthesia team saw and evaluated the patient and obtained written informed consent for general anesthesia. The patient's family elected to proceed as planned.

 

Next, the patient was transported to the operating room and transferred to the operating table. They were positioned for comfort and pressure points were padded. General anesthesia was induced via oral endotracheal tube which was subsequently secured to the orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:4 commissure by anesthesia. At this time general surgery completed the tracheostomy and PEG placement. Please review their operative note for details.

 

The patient's face was prepped with betadine and oral cavity prepped with peridex rinse. They were then draped in standard fashion with surgical towels and drapes. Corneal shields with tobradex ophthalmic ointment were placed.

 

A standard time-out procedure was done to verify patient name, age, date of birth, diagnosis, ASA Classification, procedure, site, side, risk of blood loss, fire score, allergies, and anesthetic type. All were in accord and the decision was made to begin the procedure.

 

10 cc of 1% lidocaine with 1:200,000 epi was infiltrated into the orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:5 lateral brow, skin overlying the NOE complexes, bilateral inferior conjunctiva, and maxillary vestibule.

 

Attention was then directed to the orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:6 lateral brow, where a 2-cm curvilinear incision was made through skin with a 15 blade. Careful sharp dissection was carried to the bone with bovie electrocautery. Subperiosteal dissection performed and ZF fracture was identified and noted to be displaced. A moist gauze was then placed in the incision.

 

Attention was then directed to the orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:7 lower lid, which was everted with desmarres retractors. With the globe protected with a corneal shield and ribbon retractors, a transconjunctival incision was made with bovie electrocautery, staying lateral to the lacrimal punctum. The incision was carried to the infraorbital rim and subperiosteal dissection was completed, exposing the entirety of the rim and along the orbital floor to expose the posterior bony stop.

 

Attention was then directed to the orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:8 lower lid, which was exposed using a transconjunctival approach in identical fashion.

 

Attention was then directed towards the nasofrontal junction and NOE fractures. Bilateral Lynch incisions were created in a natural skin crease staying 1 cm away from the medial canthus bilaterally. The curvilinear skin incision was created with a 15 blade. Careful sharp incision was carried down to periosteum with bovie electrocautery. Subperiosteal dissection was used to expose the comminuted NOE complex and fractures. For improved access, the Lynch incisions were connected via a horizontal incision across the nasofrontal junction crease using a 15 blade. Subperiosteal dissection completed to better expose the fractures. Hemostasis achieved and the site was packed.

 

Attention was then directed intraorally. A maxillary Keen incision was made 5 mm superior to the mucogingival junction with Bovie from first molar to first molar, with care not to disturb Stensen's duct, and carried through the periosteum. A subperiosteal dissection was carried superiorly to expose bilateral infraorbital foramen and lateral to zygomaxillary sutures. The Le Fort II fractures were exposed bilaterally.

 

Attention was then directed to the orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:9 ZF suture. Using a Boies elevator intraorally, the ZMC segment was reduced and the zygomaticofrontal fracture was properly reduced. A KLS orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:2-hole orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:text:4 plate was fixated using orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:3 orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:measurement:0-mm screws at the superior lateral orbital rim under copious irrigation. Proper reduction was confirmed.

 

Attention was then directed to the lower lid incision. All orbital contents were retracted superiorly and the inferior orbital rim was exposed with inferior retraction. A KLS orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:text:5 orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:4-hole straight plate was secured to the rim with orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:5 orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:measurement:1-mm screws under copious irrigation. Proper reduction was confirmed.

 

Attention was then directed to the intraoral incision. The orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:10 zygomaxillary fracture was again confirmed to be properly reduced and noted to be stable. A KLS orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:text:6 orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:6-hole L-plate was fixated using orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:7 orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:measurement:2-mm screws at the zygomaxillary suture fracture. Proper reduction was confirmed. The orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:11 zygomaticomaxillary fracture was fixated in identical fashion. The orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:12 piriform rim was reduced and fixated with KLS orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:text:7 orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:8-hole L-plate and orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:9 orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:measurement:3-mm screws. The orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:13 piriform rim was reduced and fixated with KLS orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:text:8 orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:10-hole plate and orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:11 orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:measurement:4-mm screws. The maxilla was noted to be stable and occlusion was native and repeatable.

 

Attention directed towards the bilateral NOE fractures. The nasofrontal junction fracture was fixated with a KLS orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:text:9 orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:12-hole Y-plate and orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:13 orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:measurement:5-mm screws under copious irrigation. The orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:14 comminuted NOE fracture was reduced with a Boies elevator in the orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:15 nare and fixated with a KLS orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:text:10 orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:14-hole curvilinear plate and orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:15 monocortical screws. At this time, telescoping of the nasal bones was noted, which was reduced and fixated with a KLS orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:text:11 orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:16-hole straight plate and orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:hardwareCount:17 monocortical screws. Appropriate canthal width and nasal projection was achieved.

 

Attention then directed to the orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:16 orbital floor which had a defect measuring orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:measurement:6 x orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:measurement:7 mm. Lateral, medial, and posterior subperiosteal dissection was performed to re-suspend orbital contents and fat from the sinus and identify the posterior stop. A KLS Resorb-X plate was trimmed and molded to cover the defect, ensuring no orbital contents were entrapped underneath. A forced duction exam was performed bilaterally and extraocular movements were noted to be completely intact.

 

All surgical sites were copiously irrigated with sterile saline. Hemostasis confirmed.

 

The lateral brow incision was closed in multiple layers.  The deep layer was closed with 3-0 vicryl, the epidermal layer was closed with 5-0 plain gut superficially.  The orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:17 transconjunctival incision was closed loosely with 5-0 plain gut with two interrupted sutures. The orif-noe-le-fort-zmc-orbit:side:18 transconjunctival incision was well-approximated without sutures placed.

 

The H-incision was closed in multiple layers. 3-0 deep vicryls were placed to re-approximate the muscle and to cover the hardware in a simple interrupted fashion. 4-0 Monocryl was used to approximate the deep dermal layer in simple, interrupted fashion. The skin was then closed with 5-0 fast gut in a simple, running fashion.

 

The intraoral incision was closed with 3-0 chromic gut in a simple, running fashion.

 

The cutaneous incisions were cleaned and dressed with dermabond and steristrips.

 

The nasal bones were once again re-evaluated and ensured to be reduced. An external nasal splint was placed.

 

This concluded the surgical procedure and the care of the patient was turned over to the anesthesia team. The patient was left with the tracheostomy tube placed, sedated, and transferred to the ICU.

 

The procedure was uncomplicated and the patient remained hemodynamically stable throughout, tolerating both the procedure and anesthesia well.
