rawBody:
PROCEDURE: ORIF NOE, Le Fort, ZMC, Orbit

 

Findings: [R/L] ZMC fracture mobile and displaced. ZF accessed via lateral brow approach, fixated with KLS [profile] plate and [#] monocortical screws. [R/L] infraorbital rim exposed via transconjunctival approach, fixated with KLS [profile] plate and [#] monocortical screws. Bilateral Le Fort II fractures exposed via Keen incision. ZM and piriform rims fixated with [profile] KLS L-plates and monocortical screws. Bilateral NOE fractures accessed via [R/L] transconjunctival approach and two connecting Lynch incisions (H-incision), noted to be severely comminuted and depressed. NOE fractures fixated with multiple KLS [profile] plates and monocortical screws. [R/L] orbital floor reconstruction completed with KLS Resorb-X plate. Bilateral forced duction tests completed with EOMI. Bilateral IO nerves visualized and noted to be intact throughout.

Technique:

The patient was brought to the pre-operative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient's family. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The anesthesia team saw and evaluated the patient and obtained written informed consent for general anesthesia. The patient's family elected to proceed as planned.

 

Next, the patient was transported to the operating room and transferred to the operating table. They were positioned for comfort and pressure points were padded. General anesthesia was induced via oral endotracheal tube which was subsequently secured to the [R/L] commissure by anesthesia. At this time general surgery completed the tracheostomy and PEG placement. Please review their operative note for details.

 

The patient's face was prepped with betadine and oral cavity prepped with peridex rinse. They were then draped in standard fashion with surgical towels and drapes. Corneal shields with tobradex ophthalmic ointment were placed.

 

A standard time-out procedure was done to verify patient name, age, date of birth, diagnosis, ASA Classification, procedure, site, side, risk of blood loss, fire score, allergies, and anesthetic type. All were in accord and the decision was made to begin the procedure.

 

10 cc of 1% lidocaine with 1:200,000 epi was infiltrated into the [R/L] lateral brow, skin overlying the NOE complexes, bilateral inferior conjunctiva, and maxillary vestibule.

 

Attention was then directed to the [R/L] lateral brow, where a 2-cm curvilinear incision was made through skin with a 15 blade. Careful sharp dissection was carried to the bone with bovie electrocautery. Subperiosteal dissection performed and ZF fracture was identified and noted to be displaced. A moist gauze was then placed in the incision.

 

Attention was then directed to the [R/L] lower lid, which was everted with desmarres retractors. With the globe protected with a corneal shield and ribbon retractors, a transconjunctival incision was made with bovie electrocautery, staying lateral to the lacrimal punctum. The incision was carried to the infraorbital rim and subperiosteal dissection was completed, exposing the entirety of the rim and along the orbital floor to expose the posterior bony stop.

 

Attention was then directed to the [R/L] lower lid, which was exposed using a transconjunctival approach in identical fashion.

 

Attention was then directed towards the nasofrontal junction and NOE fractures. Bilateral Lynch incisions were created in a natural skin crease staying 1 cm away from the medial canthus bilaterally. The curvilinear skin incision was created with a 15 blade. Careful sharp incision was carried down to periosteum with bovie electrocautery. Subperiosteal dissection was used to expose the comminuted NOE complex and fractures. For improved access, the Lynch incisions were connected via a horizontal incision across the nasofrontal junction crease using a 15 blade. Subperiosteal dissection completed to better expose the fractures. Hemostasis achieved and the site was packed.

 

Attention was then directed intraorally. A maxillary Keen incision was made 5 mm superior to the mucogingival junction with Bovie from first molar to first molar, with care not to disturb Stensen's duct, and carried through the periosteum. A subperiosteal dissection was carried superiorly to expose bilateral infraorbital foramen and lateral to zygomaxillary sutures. The Le Fort II fractures were exposed bilaterally.

 

Attention was then directed to the [R/L] ZF suture. Using a Boies elevator intraorally, the ZMC segment was reduced and the zygomaticofrontal fracture was properly reduced. A KLS [#]-hole [profile] plate was fixated using [#] [length]-mm screws at the superior lateral orbital rim under copious irrigation. Proper reduction was confirmed.

 

Attention was then directed to the lower lid incision. All orbital contents were retracted superiorly and the inferior orbital rim was exposed with inferior retraction. A KLS [profile] [#]-hole straight plate was secured to the rim with [#] [length]-mm screws under copious irrigation. Proper reduction was confirmed.

 

Attention was then directed to the intraoral incision. The [R/L] zygomaxillary fracture was again confirmed to be properly reduced and noted to be stable. A KLS [profile] [#]-hole L-plate was fixated using [#] [length]-mm screws at the zygomaxillary suture fracture. Proper reduction was confirmed. The [R/L] zygomaticomaxillary fracture was fixated in identical fashion. The [R/L] piriform rim was reduced and fixated with KLS [profile] [#]-hole L-plate and [#] [length]-mm screws. The [R/L] piriform rim was reduced and fixated with KLS [profile] [#]-hole plate and [#] [length]-mm screws. The maxilla was noted to be stable and occlusion was native and repeatable.

 

Attention directed towards the bilateral NOE fractures. The nasofrontal junction fracture was fixated with a KLS [profile] [#]-hole Y-plate and [#] [length]-mm screws under copious irrigation. The [R/L] comminuted NOE fracture was reduced with a Boies elevator in the [R/L] nare and fixated with a KLS [profile] [#]-hole curvilinear plate and [#] monocortical screws. At this time, telescoping of the nasal bones was noted, which was reduced and fixated with a KLS [profile] [#]-hole straight plate and [#] monocortical screws. Appropriate canthal width and nasal projection was achieved.

 

Attention then directed to the [R/L] orbital floor which had a defect measuring [X] x [X] mm. Lateral, medial, and posterior subperiosteal dissection was performed to re-suspend orbital contents and fat from the sinus and identify the posterior stop. A KLS Resorb-X plate was trimmed and molded to cover the defect, ensuring no orbital contents were entrapped underneath. A forced duction exam was performed bilaterally and extraocular movements were noted to be completely intact.

 

All surgical sites were copiously irrigated with sterile saline. Hemostasis confirmed.

 

The lateral brow incision was closed in multiple layers.  The deep layer was closed with 3-0 vicryl, the epidermal layer was closed with 5-0 plain gut superficially.  The [R/L] transconjunctival incision was closed loosely with 5-0 plain gut with two interrupted sutures. The [R/L] transconjunctival incision was well-approximated without sutures placed.

 

The H-incision was closed in multiple layers. 3-0 deep vicryls were placed to re-approximate the muscle and to cover the hardware in a simple interrupted fashion. 4-0 Monocryl was used to approximate the deep dermal layer in simple, interrupted fashion. The skin was then closed with 5-0 fast gut in a simple, running fashion.

 

The intraoral incision was closed with 3-0 chromic gut in a simple, running fashion.

 

The cutaneous incisions were cleaned and dressed with dermabond and steristrips.

 

The nasal bones were once again re-evaluated and ensured to be reduced. An external nasal splint was placed.

 

This concluded the surgical procedure and the care of the patient was turned over to the anesthesia team. The patient was left with the tracheostomy tube placed, sedated, and transferred to the ICU.

 

The procedure was uncomplicated and the patient remained hemodynamically stable throughout, tolerating both the procedure and anesthesia well.

title: ORIF Orbital Floor
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/01 Trauma/ORIF Orbital Floor.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: ORIF Orbital Floor 

 

Findings: orif-orbital-floor:side:0 orbital floor fracture with orbital contents herniating into the maxillary sinus. Pre-operative forced duction test showed restriction in upward gaze. Orbital floor and inferior orbital rim accessed via lower lid skin crease incision. Orbital contents elevated. Pre-formed Synthes orbital floor reconstruction plate placed across the defect and secured to the inferior orbital rim with orif-orbital-floor:hardwareCount:0 orif-orbital-floor:measurement:0 mm self-drilling screws. Posterior stop identified at orif-orbital-floor:measurement:1 mm from orbital rim. Infraorbital nerve not visualized and protected throughout. Post-operative forced duction test showed normal range of motion with EOMI.

 

The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The patient was escorted to the operating room and placed in supine position on the operating table. Anesthesia monitors were placed. The patient was placed under general anesthesia and intubated via a nasal endotracheal route without incident. The patient was then prepped and draped for surgery in the usual sterile fashion.

 

Attention was then drawn to the orif-orbital-floor:side:1 orbit which was marked and grossly edematous. A forced duction test was performed showing a true restriction in upward gaze. Corneal shields with ophthalmic lubricant were then placed into the orif-orbital-floor:side:2 eye. Next an incision was marked on the lower lid well below about 7 mm below the lower lid in a skin crease. 3 cc of 2% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epi was placed within the surgical site. The incision was made through skin and orbicularis oculi with a #15 blade, and a needle tip bovie was used to achieve adequate hemostasis. Blunt dissection was then carried down to the inferior orbital rim and once at the level of the periosteum, a sharp incision was made down to bone. A #9 periosteal elevator was used to dissect and expose the orbital floor and inferior orbital rim in a subperiosteal plane. The infraorbital nerve was not visualized and remained protected throughout the procedure. The large orbital floor fracture/defect was identified with orbital contents herniating into the maxillary sinus. The orbital contents were then gently and atraumatically elevated back into the orbit and native un-fractured and stable orbital bone was exposed on all sides of the fracture defect. The posterior stop measured to be orif-orbital-floor:measurement:2 mm from the orbital rim. With all of the orbital tissues retracted superiorly, a pre-formed Synthes orbital floor reconstruction plate was measured, cut and tried in for size. Once the plate was well-adapted across the orbital floor defect, perched on solid bone, and all orbital contents were elevated superiorly, the reconstructive plate was secured to the inferior orbital rim utilizing orif-orbital-floor:hardwareCount:1 self-drilling orif-orbital-floor:measurement:3 mm screws. The plate was noted to be well secured and stable. Next the corneal shield was removed and a forced duction test was performed with findings of a normal range of motion without restriction or impingement in any direction of gaze. The surgical site was then thoroughly irrigated with sterile saline and noted to be hemostatic and without debris. Next periosteum was closed over the plate with several simple interrupted 5-0 vicryl sutures in a buried nature. Next the skin was closed with a running 4-0 plain gut suture. Ophthalmic sterile saline was then used to irrigate the eye and a final forced duction test was completed showing extraocular movements intact.

 

This completed the surgical case. The patient was turned over to the anesthesia team, where she was extubated without complications and transferred to the PACU in stable condition.

title: ORIF Orbital Floor Custom
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/01 Trauma/ORIF Orbital Floor Custom.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: ORIF Orbital Floor Custom

 

Findings:  orif-orbital-floor-custom:measurement:0 % orbital floor fracture with inferiorly displaced orbital content noted. Custom orbital implant placed as planned with orif-orbital-floor-custom:hardwareCount:0x orif-orbital-floor-custom:measurement:1mm screw fixation. Post-operative forced duction test showed normal range of motion with EOMI.



 

The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The patient was escorted to the operating room and placed in supine position on the operating table. Anesthesia monitors were placed. The patient was placed under general anesthesia and intubated via a nasal endotracheal route without incident. The patient was then prepped and draped for surgery in the usual sterile fashion.

 

Attention was then drawn to the orif-orbital-floor-custom:side:0 orbit which was marked and grossly edematous. A forced duction test was performed showing a true restriction in upward gaze. Corneal shields with ophthalmic lubricant were then placed into the orif-orbital-floor-custom:side:1 eye. Next an incision was marked on the lower lid well below about 7 mm below the lower lid in a skin crease. 3 cc of 2% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epi was placed within the surgical site. A standard post-septal transconjunctival incision was made with Colorado tip bovie electrocautery, utilizing DesMarres retractors to gently hold the lower lid inferiorly. Electrocautery proceeded until the orbital rim was identified. The lower eyelid was retracted inferiorly and a malleable retractor used to retract the forniceal tissue over the orbital rim. An incision was created through periosteum at the orbital rim with monopolar cautery.

A periosteal elevator was then used to dissect in a subperiosteal plane along the infraorbital rim, and the fracture was visualized with herniated orbital content into the maxillary sinus. Dissection was carried posteriorly first laterally and then medially. Dissection was continued until the posterior aspect of the displaced orbital floor was encountered. Nearing the apex of the orbit, a posterior stop was encountered. At this time the extraconal fat and contents of the orbit was gently retracted superiorly. The patient specific KLS custom orbital floor reconstruction plate was inserted, taking care to ensure it was subperiosteal lateral margin. After verifying that the plate was completely seated in the anterior, medial and lateral bony walls, orif-orbital-floor-custom:hardwareCount:1x orif-orbital-floor-custom:measurement:2mm screw was used to secure the plate onto the orbital rim. Once secured and ensuring that the surgical site was hemostatic, and intraoperative C arm x-ray was performed to verify plate position.

The x-ray showed no evidence of encroachment on the orbital apex and the custom plate was in appropriate position. Throughout this process the patient did not become bradycardic or have significant cardiac changes.

The lower eyelid site was irrigated with copious sterile normal saline, and the conjunctiva was closed with tacking interrupted 2x 5-0 plain gut suture with buried knots. Corneal shield of the orif-orbital-floor-custom:side:2 eye was removed, and forced duction test performed and showed intact extra-ocular movements.

The area around both eyes was cleaned off with sterile wet and dry gauze and cleaned with BSS (balanced salt solution).

 

This completed the surgical case. The patient was turned over to the anesthesia team, where she was extubated without complications and transferred to the PACU in stable condition.

title: ORIF Symphysis
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/01 Trauma/ORIF Symphysis.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: ORIF Symphysis

 

Findings: Anterior mandibular fracture with soft tissue ingrowth identified. Fracture accessed via anterior mandibular vestibular incision. Mental nerves not visualized but protected. Fracture reduced and fixated with a orif-symphysis:hardwareCount:0-hole orif-symphysis:text:0 Biomet plate at the inferior border and a orif-symphysis:text:1 Biomet tension plate superiorly. Maxillomandibular fixation (MMF) placed and released. Stable, repeatable occlusion confirmed.

 

The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The patient was escorted to the operating room and placed in supine position on the operating table. Anesthesia monitors were placed. The patient was placed under general anesthesia and intubated via a nasal endotracheal route without incident. The patient was then draped per sterile protocol. The patient was given 2g ancef IV prior to incision.

 

Attention was then directed to the oral cavity. The oral cavity was suctioned free of secretions and debris and a throat pack was placed. 10 cc of 1% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epi was infiltrated into the maxillary and mandibular vestibules. Using a 24 G wire, a bridle wire was placed from tooth orif-symphysis:toothNumber:0 to level the mandibular occlusion. Maxillary and mandibular arch bars were placed with 24G wire from 2nd pre-molar to 2nd pre-molar. The arches were then placed into MMF. Occlusion was verified.

 

Attention was then drawn to the anterior mandibular vestibule. Using a #15 blade, an incision was made 15 mm anterior to the MGJ from orif-symphysis:toothNumber:1. The incision extended to the mandibular bone. Using a periosteal elevator, a subperiosteal dissection was completed exposing the entire fracture and the inferior border. The mental nerves were not visualized. The soft tissue ingrowth was then removed at the fracture site. The site was copiously irrigated. Using a 702 bur, two osteotomies were placed to engage bone reduction forceps to achieve proper reduction of the fracture. A orif-symphysis:hardwareCount:1-hole, orif-symphysis:text:2 Biomet plate was placed at the inferior border with orif-symphysis:hardwareCount:2 screws. In addition, a orif-symphysis:text:3 Biomet plate was placed as a tension plate superior to the previous plate with orif-symphysis:hardwareCount:3 screws.

 

The mandible was observed to be stable. Copious irrigation was used. The mentalis muscle was reapproximated with 3-0 vicryl sutures and the mucosa was closed with 3-0 vicryl in a running fashion. The patient was released from MMF and his occlusion was stable and repeatable in a passive fashion.

 

The patient’s oropharynx was then irrigated and suctioned free of debris and throat pack removed. The patient was then awakened and extubated without incidence. The patient was transported to the PACU for recovery and was noted to have stable vital signs and spontaneous respirations on arrival.

PROCEDURE: ORIF Symphysis

 

Findings: Anterior mandibular fracture with soft tissue ingrowth identified. Fracture accessed via anterior mandibular vestibular incision. Mental nerves not visualized but protected. Fracture reduced and fixated with a [#]-hole [profile] Biomet plate at the inferior border and a [profile] Biomet tension plate superiorly. Maxillomandibular fixation (MMF) placed and released. Stable, repeatable occlusion confirmed.

 

The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The patient was escorted to the operating room and placed in supine position on the operating table. Anesthesia monitors were placed. The patient was placed under general anesthesia and intubated via a nasal endotracheal route without incident. The patient was then draped per sterile protocol. The patient was given 2g ancef IV prior to incision.

 

Attention was then directed to the oral cavity. The oral cavity was suctioned free of secretions and debris and a throat pack was placed. 10 cc of 1% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epi was infiltrated into the maxillary and mandibular vestibules. Using a 24 G wire, a bridle wire was placed from tooth [#__] to level the mandibular occlusion. Maxillary and mandibular arch bars were placed with 24G wire from 2nd pre-molar to 2nd pre-molar. The arches were then placed into MMF. Occlusion was verified.

 

Attention was then drawn to the anterior mandibular vestibule. Using a #15 blade, an incision was made 15 mm anterior to the MGJ from [#__]. The incision extended to the mandibular bone. Using a periosteal elevator, a subperiosteal dissection was completed exposing the entire fracture and the inferior border. The mental nerves were not visualized. The soft tissue ingrowth was then removed at the fracture site. The site was copiously irrigated. Using a 702 bur, two osteotomies were placed to engage bone reduction forceps to achieve proper reduction of the fracture. A [#]-hole, [profile] Biomet plate was placed at the inferior border with [#] screws. In addition, a [profile] Biomet plate was placed as a tension plate superior to the previous plate with [#] screws.

 

The mandible was observed to be stable. Copious irrigation was used. The mentalis muscle was reapproximated with 3-0 vicryl sutures and the mucosa was closed with 3-0 vicryl in a running fashion. The patient was released from MMF and his occlusion was stable and repeatable in a passive fashion.

 

The patient’s oropharynx was then irrigated and suctioned free of debris and throat pack removed. The patient was then awakened and extubated without incidence. The patient was transported to the PACU for recovery and was noted to have stable vital signs and spontaneous respirations on arrival.

title: ORIF ZMC
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/01 Trauma/ORIF ZMC.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: ORIF ZMC

 

Findings: orif-zmc:side:0 zygomaticofrontal, zygomaticomaxillary, and zygomatic arch fractures identified and manually reduced. orif-zmc:side:1 ZF suture exposed via lateral brow incision and fixated with a Synthes orif-zmc:hardwareCount:0-hole plate and orif-zmc:measurement:0 mm screws. orif-zmc:side:2 ZM suture and anterior maxillary sinus fracture exposed via maxillary vestibular incision and fixated with a Synthes orif-zmc:text:0 profile L-plate and orif-zmc:measurement:1 mm screws. Adequate reduction confirmed.

 

The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The patient was escorted to the operating room and placed in supine position on the operating table. Anesthesia monitors were placed. The patient was placed under general anesthesia and intubated via a nasal endotracheal route without incident. The oral cavity was suctioned and a throat pack was placed and the mouth was rinsed with peridex. The patient was then prepped and draped for surgery in the usual sterile fashion.

 

Attention was directed to the orif-zmc:side:3 lateral brow where the patient was anesthetized with 2 cc of 1% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epi. Using a 15 blade, a curvilinear incision was performed along the lateral orbital wall. Subperiosteal dissection was performed to expose the zygomaticofrontal suture, which was fractured and displaced.

 

Attention was directed to the orif-zmc:side:4 maxillary vestibule where the patient was anesthetized with 3 cc 1% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epi. Using a 15 blade, an incision was made 5 mm above the mucogingival junction from midline to the first molar region of the orif-zmc:side:5 maxilla. The incision was carried to periosteum and a subperiosteal dissection followed, exposing the anterior maxillary sinus fracture and zygomaticomaxillary suture fracture. The site was copiously irrigated. The ZF, ZM, and zygomatic arch fractures were manually reduced with rowe disimpaction forceps. The ZF suture was fixated using a synthes curvilinear orif-zmc:text:1 profile orif-zmc:hardwareCount:1-hole plate, secured with self-drilling orif-zmc:measurement:2 mm screws. The ZM was fixated with a Synthes orif-zmc:text:2 profile L-plate, secured with orif-zmc:hardwareCount:2 self drilling orif-zmc:measurement:3 mm screws. Adequate reduction was confirmed. The incisions and surgical sites was irrigated with normal saline and hemostasis was confirmed.

 

The lateral brow incision was closed with 3-0 deep vicryls, 4-0 subdermal monocryls, and 5-0 plain gut for the skin. Steri-strips placed overlying the incision. The intraoral incision was closed with 3-0 vicryl running suture. The oral cavity was irrigated, suctioned and the throat pack was removed. All counts were correct.

 

This concluded the surgical procedure. Care of the patient was then turned over to the anesthesia team. The patient was extubated in the operating room without incident. The patient was transferred from the operating room to the recovery room with stable vital signs and spontaneous respirations verified.

title: Otoplasty
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/06 Cosmetic & Facial/Otoplasty.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: Otoplasty

 

FINDINGS: Bilateral prominent ears. Left and right conchal bowls and helices accessed via postauricular incisions with otoplasty:measurement:0 mm skin excisions. Kidney bean-shaped conchal cartilage excised bilaterally, preserving otoplasty:measurement:1 mm conchal depth. Mustarde sutures placed to re-create antihelical folds. Mastoid fascia sutured to subperichondrial layer. Bilateral pressure dressings applied. Final ear measurements otoplasty:measurement:2-otoplasty:measurement:3-otoplasty:measurement:4 bilaterally.

 

The patient was greeted in the main operating room holding area where identification was verified, health history updated and any questions presented were answered to apparent satisfaction.  Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified, surgical site was verified, and the patient expressed desires to proceed as planned. All questions were answered. Anesthesia evaluated and deemed the patient appropriate to proceed. Anesthesia transported the patient to the operating room via gurney.

 

The patient was placed on the operating room table in the supine position, secured with a security strap, sequential compression devices were placed, and pressure points padded.   General anesthesia was induced and a successful oral-endotracheal intubation was achieved. The eyes were secured closed with steri-strips. The patient was then prepped and draped in the usual and sterile fashion for a surgical procedure of the head/neck. Blue towel was placed to isolate the oral cavity from peri-orbital and bilateral ear area. A surgical time-out was completed.  

 

TECHNIQUE:

Attention was directed to the left ear. Utilizing a surgical marking pen, the proposed postauricular incision was designed with a otoplasty:measurement:5 mm width skin excision. The kidney bean shaped piece of cartilage planned for excision was marked as well, measuring otoplasty:measurement:6 mm of conchal cartilage preserved. 2% Lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine was administered in the subcutaneous plane for hemostasis and hydrodissection. A surgical blade was used to create a linear incision through skin and into the subcutaneous tissue in the posterior auricular crease. Dissection was carried down to the subperichondrial plane. The 3mm strip of skin was excised across the length of the incision. The subperiochondral plane was identified and sharp dissection exposed the conchal bowl and helix. Electrocautery was then used to excise subcutaneous tissue in the mastoid region, to establish a soft tissue bed of mastoid fascia for conchal bowl adaptation. Hemostasis was obtained with electrocautery.

 

Multiple 30 gauge hypodermic needle was passed through the cartilage from anterior to posterior into the surgical wound, demarcating the base of the conchal bowl from the anterior aspect of the ear. A kidney bean shaped piece of conchal bowl cartilage, otoplasty:measurement:7 mm in width, was excised ensuring  otoplasty:measurement:8 mm of conchal depth was remaining.

 

30 gauge needles were passed from anterior to posterior to place Mustarde sutures in a horizontal mattress fashion in order to re-create/accentuate the antihelical fold. Two of these sutures were placed in a similar fashion utilizing 4-0 Mersilene.  

 

3-0 Vicryl was used to suture the subperichondrial layer to the mastoid fascia in horizontal mattress and interrupted fashion. The lateral projection of the helix was then verified with a ruler and measured. The surgical site was irrigated with copious normal sterile saline. Additional 3-0 Vicryl was placed to close the subcutaneous layer. The skin was closed with running 5-0 plain gut suture, leaving otoplasty:measurement:9 mm at the apex for drainage.

 

Blu-mousse was used to impress the concha bowl as pressure dressing with Xeroform was placed in each conchal bowl. Xeroform gauze was then placed over the posterior surgical site as well.

The right ear was completed in a similar fashion. A Barton headwrap was then secured as dressing.

 

 Final measurement from Superior to inferior:

 LEFT: otoplasty:measurement:10-otoplasty:measurement:11-otoplasty:measurement:12

 RIGHT: otoplasty:measurement:13-otoplasty:measurement:14-otoplasty:measurement:15

 

The patient’s face was cleansed with moistened lap pads and the head wrap dressing was removed. The eyes were rinsed with BSS. The general anesthesia was reversed, and patient was extubated in the operating room.  The patient tolerated the surgery and the anesthesia well, and was transported to the Post-Anesthesia Care Unit where normal stable vital signs were maintained. There were no procedural or anesthetic complications.

title: SARPE
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/04 Orthognathic/SARPE.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: SARPE

 

Findings: Maxillary transverse deficiency. Bilateral Le Fort osteotomies completed from maxillary buttress to piriform rim. Interdental osteotomy performed between teeth sarpe:toothNumber:0 and sarpe:toothNumber:1. Septum and lateral nasal walls separated. Hyrax appliance activated with sarpe:measurement:0 turns (sarpe:measurement:1 mm expansion) to confirm symmetric expansion and then reduced back to sarpe:measurement:2 turns (sarpe:measurement:3 mm). Interferences reduced. Bone morphogenic protein (BMP) placed into the interdental osteotomy. Bilateral infraorbital neurovascular bundles visualized and intact.

 

The patient was met in the preoperative holding area. The plan for the surgery was reviewed with the patient. The history and physical examination was updated. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered.

The patient was then transported to the operating room and placed in the supine position on the operating room table. General anesthesia was then induced with the anesthesia team via the intravenous route, and the airway was secured with a nasoendotracheal tube. Pressure points were padded and checked. At this time, the oropharynx was suctioned free of debris and a moist throat pack was placed. The maxillary surgical site was anesthetized with 10cc lidocaine 2% with 1:100,000 epinephrine with local infiltration. The patient was prepped and draped in sterile fashion for an orthognathic procedure. A surgical time-out was performed. Orthodontic brackets and lugs counted and verified by 2x people of the surgical team.

 

Attention was then directed to the patient’s maxillary buccal vestibule where a circumvestibular incision was initiated using bovie electrocautery starting at the mucosa of the left first molar approximately 5 mm superior to the mucogingival junction to the area of the right first molar. This incision was then carried down to bone. Periosteal elevators were used to reflect a full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap that extended from the right maxillary buttress to the left maxillary buttress. The flap was also extended superiorly to expose the right and left piriform rims and the right and left infraorbital neurovascular bundles. Both right and left neurovascular bundles were noted to be intact and continued to remain so for the duration of the case.

 

Attention was directed to the patient’s nasal complex where dissection of the nasal mucosa was performed with a curved free elevator. A Sayer elevator was used to elevate the nasal mucosa from the bone. Similar soft tissue dissection was carried out on both the right and left sides. Next, the reciprocating saw was used to mark Le Fort osteotomy sites from the right and left curvature of the maxillary buttress to the piriform rim in a flat and symmetric nature staying clear of critical structures (tooth roots). The Le Fort osteotomy was then finished with a reciprocating saw on both the right and left maxilla—as planned taking a conservative wedge of bone off the lateral zygomaticomaxillary junction to avoid interferences with expansion. An interdental osteotomy was initiated between teeth sarpe:toothNumber:2/sarpe:toothNumber:3 with an oscillating saw under copious irrigation. A double safe-sided osteotome was used to separate the septum from the palate. Single safe-sided osteotomes were used to separate the lateral nasal walls. A straight osteotome was used to complete the osteotomy between sarpe:toothNumber:4 and sarpe:toothNumber:5 which was also extended posteriorly to the posterior extent of the hard palate. The Hyrax appliance was then activated with sarpe:measurement:4 turns (sarpe:measurement:5 mm of expansion). Symmetric and appropriate expansion was noted and any relevant interferences were conservatively reduced. The appliance was then reduced back sarpe:measurement:6 turns (leaving a total of sarpe:measurement:7 mm expansion). The intraoral soft tissues of the palate were inspected with no tears noted. The segments were confirmed to be moving equally and independently with the same turning algorithm previously noted, leaving a total of sarpe:measurement:8 turns activated at the end of the case (sarpe:measurement:9 mm). Bone morphogenic protein which had been hydrated for 20 minutes on an absorbable collagen sponge was placed into the interdental osteotomy after a final thorough irrigation.

 

Closure:

The maxillary surgical site had been thoroughly irrigated with a copious amount of sterile normal saline and grafted as noted previously with BMP. Attention was then directed toward closure. The mucosa was tacked closed at the midline with a 3-0 chromic gut suture. The circumvestibular incision of the maxilla was then closed bilaterally with a running 3-0 chromic gut suture.

 

Of note, before final closure: Orthodontic brackets and lugs counted and verified by 2x people of the surgical team. Counts were correct and verified by the surgical team, confirming with the OR nurse.

 

At this time, the oropharynx was irrigated and suctioned free of debris, and the moist throat pack was removed. An orogastric tube was passed, decompressing the stomach. Occlusion was then rechecked and confirmed appropriately. Patient was placed into bilateral guiding elastics. 10 cc of Exparel was injected at maxillary and mandibular surgical sites via local infiltration. The face was cleaned with moist Ray-Tecs. The head wrap was removed and the patient was turned over to the anesthesia team. A JawBra was placed with gauze fluffs in the lower facial region. The patient was extubated in the main operating room and transferred to the PACU with appropriate vital signs and spontaneous respirations, having tolerated the procedures well.

rawBody:
PROCEDURE: SARPE

 

Findings: Maxillary transverse deficiency. Bilateral Le Fort osteotomies completed from maxillary buttress to piriform rim. Interdental osteotomy performed between teeth [#__] and [#__]. Septum and lateral nasal walls separated. Hyrax appliance activated with [X] turns ([X] mm expansion) to confirm symmetric expansion and then reduced back to [X] turns ([X] mm). Interferences reduced. Bone morphogenic protein (BMP) placed into the interdental osteotomy. Bilateral infraorbital neurovascular bundles visualized and intact.

 

The patient was met in the preoperative holding area. The plan for the surgery was reviewed with the patient. The history and physical examination was updated. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered.

The patient was then transported to the operating room and placed in the supine position on the operating room table. General anesthesia was then induced with the anesthesia team via the intravenous route, and the airway was secured with a nasoendotracheal tube. Pressure points were padded and checked. At this time, the oropharynx was suctioned free of debris and a moist throat pack was placed. The maxillary surgical site was anesthetized with 10cc lidocaine 2% with 1:100,000 epinephrine with local infiltration. The patient was prepped and draped in sterile fashion for an orthognathic procedure. A surgical time-out was performed. Orthodontic brackets and lugs counted and verified by 2x people of the surgical team.

 

Attention was then directed to the patient’s maxillary buccal vestibule where a circumvestibular incision was initiated using bovie electrocautery starting at the mucosa of the left first molar approximately 5 mm superior to the mucogingival junction to the area of the right first molar. This incision was then carried down to bone. Periosteal elevators were used to reflect a full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap that extended from the right maxillary buttress to the left maxillary buttress. The flap was also extended superiorly to expose the right and left piriform rims and the right and left infraorbital neurovascular bundles. Both right and left neurovascular bundles were noted to be intact and continued to remain so for the duration of the case.

 

Attention was directed to the patient’s nasal complex where dissection of the nasal mucosa was performed with a curved free elevator. A Sayer elevator was used to elevate the nasal mucosa from the bone. Similar soft tissue dissection was carried out on both the right and left sides. Next, the reciprocating saw was used to mark Le Fort osteotomy sites from the right and left curvature of the maxillary buttress to the piriform rim in a flat and symmetric nature staying clear of critical structures (tooth roots). The Le Fort osteotomy was then finished with a reciprocating saw on both the right and left maxilla—as planned taking a conservative wedge of bone off the lateral zygomaticomaxillary junction to avoid interferences with expansion. An interdental osteotomy was initiated between teeth [#__]/[#__] with an oscillating saw under copious irrigation. A double safe-sided osteotome was used to separate the septum from the palate. Single safe-sided osteotomes were used to separate the lateral nasal walls. A straight osteotome was used to complete the osteotomy between [#__] and [#__] which was also extended posteriorly to the posterior extent of the hard palate. The Hyrax appliance was then activated with [X] turns ([X] mm of expansion). Symmetric and appropriate expansion was noted and any relevant interferences were conservatively reduced. The appliance was then reduced back [X] turns (leaving a total of [X] mm expansion). The intraoral soft tissues of the palate were inspected with no tears noted. The segments were confirmed to be moving equally and independently with the same turning algorithm previously noted, leaving a total of [X] turns activated at the end of the case ([X] mm). Bone morphogenic protein which had been hydrated for 20 minutes on an absorbable collagen sponge was placed into the interdental osteotomy after a final thorough irrigation.

 

Closure:

The maxillary surgical site had been thoroughly irrigated with a copious amount of sterile normal saline and grafted as noted previously with BMP. Attention was then directed toward closure. The mucosa was tacked closed at the midline with a 3-0 chromic gut suture. The circumvestibular incision of the maxilla was then closed bilaterally with a running 3-0 chromic gut suture.

 

Of note, before final closure: Orthodontic brackets and lugs counted and verified by 2x people of the surgical team. Counts were correct and verified by the surgical team, confirming with the OR nurse.

 

At this time, the oropharynx was irrigated and suctioned free of debris, and the moist throat pack was removed. An orogastric tube was passed, decompressing the stomach. Occlusion was then rechecked and confirmed appropriately. Patient was placed into bilateral guiding elastics. 10 cc of Exparel was injected at maxillary and mandibular surgical sites via local infiltration. The face was cleaned with moist Ray-Tecs. The head wrap was removed and the patient was turned over to the anesthesia team. A JawBra was placed with gauze fluffs in the lower facial region. The patient was extubated in the main operating room and transferred to the PACU with appropriate vital signs and spontaneous respirations, having tolerated the procedures well.

title: Septoplasty + Inferior Turbinate Reduction (Functional)
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/06 Cosmetic & Facial/Septorhinoplasty (Functional).md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: Septoplasty + Inferior Turbinate Reduction (Functional)



Findings: 

Septoplasty  inferior turbinoplasty - Septum significantly deviated to the left, right sided compensated turbinate hypertrophy. Killian incision to L septum and nasal septum subperichondral dissection on left side of septum. Encountered bone spur. Dorsal and caudal 1cm strut identified and deviated portions of the septal cartilage and bone spurs were removed. Collagen gel foam placed at region of septoplasty. Septum straightened, 4-0 interrupted chromic gut suture closed killian incision. 4-0 quilting suture used to compress interseptal space. Septal perforation noted on posterior aspect of septum near maxillary crest. Stab incision to R inferior turbinate, microdebrider used to reduce overall size of turbinate size while preserving mucosa.

Technique:

The patient was identified  evaluated in the pre-operating holding area. No changes were noted to the history and physical exam. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The surgical site was then marked. The patient was then taken to the operating room by the anesthesia team. He was transferred to the operating room table in the supine position. The standard ASA monitors were applied. General anesthesia was induced via IV route and an endotracheal tube placed and secured. With placement of all pressure points, the operating room table was turned 90 degrees away from the anesthesia machine. The patient was anesthetized in the septum and bilateral turbinates with 6.8cc 2% Lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine. Three pledgets were then soaked in Afrin and inserted into each nares (six pledgets total). The patient was then prepped with ophthalmic betadine and draped in the usual sterile fashion, while the surgeons scrubbed, gowned, and gloved, and a universal time out was then completed. 

Septoplasty:
A Tegaderm was used to cover the oral cavity to isolate the surgical field. Attention directed to the left nare. A Killian incision was made using an 15 blade through mucosa down to cartilage 1cm posterior to the caudal septal margin. Subperiochondrial dissection was performed with a cottle to expose the entirety of the left cartilaginous septum to the level of the maxillary crest and to the vomer posteriorly. Keeping a 10mm dorsal and caudal strut, a 15 blade was used to incise through cartilage with care to keep right sided mucosal tissue intact. Subperiochondral dissection was completed on the right aspect of the septum through the left Killian incision to roughly the same depth as the left. The septal cartilage was then harvested with a septal scissors and Takahashi forceps. The deviated portion of the bony septum was identified. With aid of a 30 degree McCain endoscope, subperiosteal dissected was completed around the deviated section along with bony spur. Blunt end of a cottle was used to fracture the deviated bony septum and was removed with a septal rongeur. Further portions of deviated bony septum were removed with Takahashi forceps. Of note there was a septal perforation on the inferior aspect of the septum near the deviated segment. 

Turbinoplasty:
Attention directed to the right nare, where the enlarged inferior turbinate was visualized. A stab incision was created with a 15 blade and submucoperiosteal dissection completed with a cottle. Microdebrider inserted into the anterior incision of inferior turbinate. A 2-mm microdebrider used to reduce the inferior turbinate. Inferior turbinate outfractured with a sayer. The left inferior turbinate was more easily visualized after septal deviation corrected and size noted to be satisfactory. Decision made to not microdebride left inferior turbinate. A sayer was used to outfracture the left inferior turbinate. 

Closure of Septoplasty: 
Next attention was directed towards closure of the septoplasty Killian incision. Gel foam flattened and shaped to roughly the shape of the septal harvest was placed. Caudal aspect of tissue was reapproximated with 4-0 Chromic gut suture in an interrupted and running fashion. Incision inferiorly over maxillary crest was reapproximated with 4-0 Chromic gut suture in an interrupted fashion. Septal perforation attempted to close with 4-0 Chromic gut suture where primary closure was unable to be achieved due to depth of perforation. A quilting stitch was placed through the septum with 4-0 plain gut suture. Doyle splints coated in bacitracin were then inserted into each nares and secured to the septum with 2-0 Silk suture, leaving long tails and tucked within the doyle splint tube. SinuFoam was then injected around the doyle splints.


The Tegaderm was removed from the oral cavity and the oropharynx was suctioned free of debris. The nasal splints were confirmed to be in good position. This concluded the surgical procedure. Care of the patient was turned over to the anesthesia team. The patient was extubated in the operating room without incident and transferred to the PACU with stable vital signs and spontaneous respirations verified, having tolerated the procedure and anesthesia well. The patient was given nasal precautions and discharged with septorhinoplasty-functional:enumText:0 instructions.

title: Sialodochoplasty (Mandible Left)
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/03 Pathology, Salivary & Infection/Sialodochoplasty (Mandible Left).md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: Sialodochoplasty (Mandible Left)

Findings: 



Transoral approach to sialodochoplasty-mandible-left:side:0 submandibular sialolith located at the Hilum. Lingual nerve identified along the lateral aspect of the incision and protected. Sialolith sialodochoplasty-mandible-left:measurement:0 cm delivered atraumatically along with additional smaller sialoliths. Duct found to be patent after removal of stone. FOM mucosa loosely reapproximated with interrupted 3-0 chromic gut

Technique:

The patient was met in the preoperative holding area. The plan for the surgery was reviewed with the patient. All patient questions were answered. The history and physical examination was updated. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered.

The patient was then transported to the operating room and placed in the supine position on the operating room table. General anesthesia was then induced with the anesthesia team and the airway was secured with a nasoendotracheal tube. Pressure points were padded and checked. At this time, the oropharynx was suctioned free of debris. The mouth had been previously prepped with Peridex and brushing. The patient was prepped and draped in sterile fashion. A surgical time-out was performed.

The floor of the mouth was anesthetized with 2 cc of lidocaine 2% with epinephrine via local infiltration. The palpable stone in the sialodochoplasty-mandible-left:side:1 proximal hilum of Wharton's duct was identified through bimanual palpation.

A trans-oral approach was then utilized with dissection through the mucosa overlying the palpable stone using electrocautery, taking the incision just through the mucosa. Blunt dissection was then performed using hemostat and nine-molt instruments, ensuring to reflect the lingual nerve and the sublingual gland to ride an avascular plane down to Wharton's duct and the stone.

The stone was visualized and carefully dissected free atraumatically and submitted as a permanent specimen fixed in formalin for pathological examination. The surgical site was then thoroughly irrigated with copious normal saline and hemostasis was achieved. The site was examined and the relevant anatomy including the lingual nerve and sublingual gland were seen to be unaltered and intact.

The surgical site was then closed loosely with tacking 3-0 chromic gut sutures in a simple interrupted fashion to allow for adequate drainage. Attention was then directed to the conclusion of the case and emergence from anesthesia.

At this time, the oropharynx was irrigated and suctioned free of debris. An orogastric tube was passed, decompressing the stomach. The face was cleaned with moist ray-tecs. The patient was turned over to the anesthesia team and was extubated in the main operating room and transferred to the PACU with appropriate vital signs and spontaneous respirations, having tolerated the procedure well.

title: Single Piece LeFort I + BSSO + Genioplasty
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/04 Orthognathic/Single Piece LeFort I and BSSO and Genioplasty.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: Single Piece LeFort I + BSSO + Genioplasty

FINDINGS: Maxillary hypoplasia, mandibular hyperplasia, and microgenia. Maxilla advanced single-piece-lefort-i-and-bsso-and-genioplasty:measurement:0 mm and impacted single-piece-lefort-i-and-bsso-and-genioplasty:measurement:1 mm. Mandible set back single-piece-lefort-i-and-bsso-and-genioplasty:measurement:2 mm. Chin advanced single-piece-lefort-i-and-bsso-and-genioplasty:measurement:3 mm. Bilateral inferior alveolar nerves identified and preserved intraoperatively. Maxillomandibular complex secured into final planned occlusion using a fabricated surgical splint.

The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The patient was escorted to the operating room and placed in the supine position on the operating table. Anesthesia monitors were placed. The patient was placed under general anesthesia and intubated with a nasal endotracheal tube without incident. The oral cavity was packed with a moistened throat pack. The face and oral cavity were prepped and draped per standard sterile protocol.

Attention was first directed to the maxilla. Local anesthetic consisting of 1% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine was infiltrated into the maxillary vestibule. A circumvestibular incision was made with a needle point bovie from first molar to first molar. Subperiosteal dissection exposed the maxilla from the piriform rims to the pterygomaxillary junctions. The planned LeFort I osteotomies were marked and completed using a reciprocating saw and piezosurgery unit. The nasal septum and lateral nasal walls were separated using osteotomes, and the maxilla was down-fractured using manual pressure. The descending palatine vessels were preserved. Bony interferences were removed. The intermediate splint was placed, and the patient was placed into MMF. The maxilla was passively seated into the planned position and secured using four L-shaped titanium miniplates and monocortical screws. MMF was released, and the intermediate splint was removed. An alar cinch suture was placed using 2-0 PDS. The maxillary incision was closed in a V-Y fashion utilizing 3-0 chromic gut.

Attention was then directed to the mandible. Local anesthetic was infiltrated along the anterior border of the rami bilaterally. An incision was made along the external oblique ridge extending to the first molar region. Subperiosteal dissection exposed the medial and lateral aspects of the ramus. The medial, sagittal, and vertical osteotomies were performed using a reciprocating saw and bur. The splits were completed carefully utilizing osteotomes and Smith spreaders, noting that the inferior alveolar nerves were bilaterally visualized and housed safely within the proximal segments.

The final surgical splint was placed, and the patient was placed into tight MMF using wire ligatures. The condyles were properly seated in the glenoid fossae. The proximal and distal segments were rigidly fixated bilaterally utilizing titanium miniplates and monocortical/bicortical screws. MMF was released, and the occlusion was checked and found to be highly reproducible into the surgical splint. The mandibular incisions were closed with 3-0 chromic gut.

Attention was finally directed to the anterior mandible. Local anesthetic was infiltrated into the anterior mandibular vestibule. An incision was made from canine to canine, well below the mucogingival junction. Dissection was carried down to the symphysis, and the mental nerves were identified and protected bilaterally. A horizontal sliding osteotomy was performed with a reciprocating saw below the apices of the teeth and the mental foramen. The inferior segment was mobilized, advanced to the planned position, and fixated rigidly using a pre-bent titanium chin plate and screws. The incision was closed in layers with 3-0 Vicryl for the mentalis muscle and 3-0 chromic gut for the mucosa.

The throat pack was removed. The oral cavity and stomach were suctioned clear of blood and debris. Guiding elastics were placed. The patient was awakened, extubated without incident, and transported to the PACU in stable condition.

An alar cinch suture was placed using 2-0 PDS. The maxillary incision was closed in a V-Y fashion utilizing 3-0 chromic gut.

Attention was then directed to the mandible. Local anesthetic was infiltrated along the anterior border of the rami bilaterally. An incision was made along the external oblique ridge extending to the first molar region. Subperiosteal dissection exposed the medial and lateral aspects of the ramus. The medial, sagittal, and vertical osteotomies were performed using a reciprocating saw and bur. The splits were completed carefully utilizing osteotomes and Smith spreaders, noting that the inferior alveolar nerves were bilaterally visualized and housed safely within the proximal segments.

The final surgical splint was placed, and the patient was placed into tight MMF using wire ligatures. The condyles were properly seated in the glenoid fossae. The proximal and distal segments were rigidly fixated bilaterally utilizing titanium miniplates and monocortical/bicortical screws. MMF was released, and the occlusion was checked and found to be highly reproducible into the surgical splint. The mandibular incisions were closed with 3-0 chromic gut.

Attention was finally directed to the anterior mandible. Local anesthetic was infiltrated into the anterior mandibular vestibule. An incision was made from canine to canine, well below the mucogingival junction. Dissection was carried down to the symphysis, and the mental nerves were identified and protected bilaterally. A horizontal sliding osteotomy was performed with a reciprocating saw below the apices of the teeth and the mental foramen. The inferior segment was mobilized, advanced to the planned position, and fixated rigidly using a pre-bent titanium chin plate and screws. The incision was closed in layers with 3-0 Vicryl for the mentalis muscle and 3-0 chromic gut for the mucosa.

The throat pack was removed. The oral cavity and stomach were suctioned clear of blood and debris. Guiding elastics were placed. The patient was awakened, extubated without incident, and transported to the PACU in stable condition.

title: Subperiosteal Maxilla + All-On-X Mandible
sourcePath: Note Writing/Op Note Templates/02 Dentoalveolar & Implant/Subperiosteal Maxilla and All-On-X Mandible.md
bodyTemplate:
PROCEDURE: Subperiosteal Maxilla + All-On-X Mandible

Findings: 

Maxillary subperiosteal implant and Mandibular All-on-X. Maxillary subperiosteal implant with subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:side:0 sinus lift/impaction. KLS maxillary subperiosteal implant
Mandibular alveoplasty. Following implants were placed:

subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:toothNumber:0: subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:hardwareDim:0 (subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:0 Ncm, subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:1 degree)
subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:toothNumber:1: subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:hardwareDim:1 (subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:2 Ncm, subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:3 degree)
subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:toothNumber:2: subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:hardwareDim:2 (subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:4 Ncm, subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:5 degree)
subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:toothNumber:3: subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:hardwareDim:3 (subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:6 Ncm, subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:7 degree)   

Multi-unit abutments fitted intra-orally. Interim maxillary prosthesis delivered with prosthodontist. No healing caps placed over abutments. Primary closure with 3-0 chromic. DBM bone grafting of maxillary and mandibular extraction sockets, covered with collagen membrane. 10cc Exparel injected end of case.

Technique:

The patient was met in the preoperative holding area. The plan for the surgery was reviewed with the patient. The history and physical examination was updated. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed with the patient. Consent was verified. All questions were answered.

The patient was then transported to the operating room and placed in the supine position on the operating room table. General anesthesia was then induced with the anesthesia team via the intravenous route, and the airway was secured with a nasoendotracheal tube. Pressure points were padded and checked. At this time, the oropharynx was suctioned free of debris. The patient was prepped and draped in sterile fashion. A surgical time-out was performed. The maxillary surgical sites were anesthetized with lidocaine 2% with 1:100,000 epinephrine with local infiltration.

Maxillary Alveoloplasty and subperiosteal implant
Attention was then directed to the patient's maxilla where a crestal incision was created along the palatal aspect of the alveolus roughly between sites #2 and #15 with distal releases bilaterally near the tuberosities toward the zygomatic process using a #15 blade. This incision was then carried down to bone and reflected in a subperiosteal plane circumferentially to expose buccal bone of maxilla. Next the flap was further reflected and extended from the right maxillary buttress to the left maxillary buttress. The flap was also extended superiorly to expose the right and left piriform rims and the right and left infraorbital neurovascular bundles. Both right and left neurovascular bundles were noted to be intact and continued to remain so for the duration of the case. Of note, the subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:side:1 lateral maxillary sinus wall was perforated during dissection due to thin bone covering the sinus near the maxillary buttress. Attention was directed to the patient's nasal complex where conservative dissection of the nasal mucosa was performed with a #9 periosteal elevator and Woodson periosteal elevator to accommodate the piriform rim tab on the cutting guide and subperiosteal implant.
Next the alveolar cutting guide was seated. Seating was noted to be passive along bilateral maxillary buttresses and the subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:side:2 piriform rim. The subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:side:3 aspect of the cut guide was noted to seat passively along the alveolar bone but with space between guide and piriform rim on the subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:side:4 side. With the cut guide seated, it was secured with temporary fixation screws. The piriform rim predictive holes were drilled under copious irrigation to the preplanned depth of the surgical plan. The alveolar bone was reduced to the level of the cutting guide with a pineapple bur and round diamond bur. The maxillary sinus membrane was noted to be exposed in the posterior aspect of the subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:side:5 alveolar bone. The sinus membrane was carefully dissected and positioned superiorly to allow passive seating of the subperiosteal implant. No perforation of the sinus membrane was noted. The temporary fixation screws were then removed along with the custom osteotomy guide. 
Next the custom KLS titanium subperiosteal implant was seated on the maxilla and noted to have a passive and well adapted fit along the bilateral maxillary buttresses and subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:side:6 piriform rim with predictive holes aligning within the fixation holes. The subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:side:7 aspect of the implant showed proper alignment of the predictive fixation holes, but was slightly elevated slightly off the bone. This discrepancy persisted after removal of any interferences along anterior nasal spine, subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:side:8 alveolar bone, or subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:side:9 alveolar bone but with predictive holes properly aligned. The subperiosteal implant was then secured to the pre-drilled maxilla with subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:8 and subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:9 monocortical screws alternating from left to right at various lengths according to the surgical plan with a subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:text:0 at the buttresses and subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:text:1 at the piriform rims. 

Closure of maxillary surgical site 
The surgical site was then irrigated with copious sterile normal saline. Attention was then turned to the subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:side:10 posterior aspect of the alveolus where the sinus was lifted. A collagen membrane was folded and placed over the sinus membrane for reinforcement. Additional collagen membranes were placed over the lateral maxillary sinus wall perforations on the subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:side:11 side. 
Attention was then turned to closure. The midline was tacked with a single interrupted 3-0 Chromic gut suture. The distal releases were closed with 3-0 chromic gut sutures in a running fashion bilaterally. The crestal incision was closed with 3-0 chromic gut suture in a running fashion. Keratinized tissue was noted to cover the implant abutments and were exposed with a 5mm tissue punch with care to maximize keratinized tissue on the buccal aspect of the abutments. Closure was reinforced with a 3-0 chromic sutures in an interrupted fashion.

Delivery of provisional prosthesis
The provisional KLS prosthesis was seated passively over the abutments with provided abutment screws and tightened to 15Ncm. The prosthesis was noted to have esthetic appearance with appropriate tooth show. 

Mandibular alveoloplasty and implants 
Attention was then directed to the patient's mandible, where 10cc of 2% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine was injected for bilateral inferior alveolar nerve blocks and locally around the alveolus. A 15 blade was used to make a crestal incision bisecting the keratinized gingiva along the alveolar ridge from the right to left posterior regions with distal buccal releases, raising a full thickness mucoperiosteal flap. The mandible was skeletonized, identifying the bilateral mental nerves, which were visualized intact and preserved throughout the procedure. The lingual gingiva was also reflected in a subperiosteal plane. A TPX handpiece fitted with a pineapple bur under copious irrigation was then used to complete mandibular alveoloplasty in both quadrants until the alveolus was parallel with the maxillary occlusal plane.

Attention was directed to implant site subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:toothNumber:4, the Nobel active system was followed to develop a subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:hardwareDim:4mm dimension osteotomy. subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:hardwareDim:5mm implant placed and torqued to subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:10 Ncm. Bone profiling was performed, a straight MUA was placed and check to ensure implant profile lined up to palatal aspect of upper anterior.

Attention was directed to implant site subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:toothNumber:5, the Nobel active system was followed to develop a subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:hardwareDim:6mm dimension osteotomy. subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:hardwareDim:7mm implant placed and torqued to subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:11 Ncm. Bone profiling was performed, a straight MUA was placed and check to ensure implant profile lined up to palatal aspect of upper anterior.

Attention was directed to implant site subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:toothNumber:6, the Nobel active system was followed to develop a subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:hardwareDim:8mm dimension osteotomy. Given the position of the mental nerve, this implant was placed at an angle. subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:hardwareDim:9mm placed and torqued to great than subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:12 Ncm. Bone profiling was performed, a subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:13 degree MUA was placed and check to ensure implant profile lined up to palatal cusps of upper posterior teeth.

Attention was directed to implant site subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:toothNumber:7, the Nobel active system was followed to develop a subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:hardwareDim:10mm dimension osteotomy. Given the position of the mental nerve this implant was placed at an angle. A subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:hardwareDim:11mm implant placed and torqued to greater than subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:14 Ncm. A subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:measurement:15 degree MUA was placed and checked to ensure implant profile lined up to palatal cusps of upper posterior teeth. 
Verification of abutment seating was completed with multiple periapical radiographs at each implant. 

Closure
The mandibular surgical sites were irrigated with copious amounts of sterile saline. Healing caps were placed on each abutment. Midline was tacked with an interrupted 3-0 Chromic gut suture. Next the crestal incision was closed with 3-0 Chromic gut suture in a running fashion. 


At this time, the oropharynx was irrigated and suctioned free of debris. An orogastric tube was passed, decompressing the stomach. 10cc of exparel was injected at maxillary and mandibular surgical sites via local infiltration. The face was cleaned with moist ray-tecs. The head wrap was removed and the patient was turned over to the anesthesia team. The lips were liberally lubricated with hydrocortisone cream. The patient was extubated in the main operating room and transferred to the PACU with appropriate vital signs and spontaneous respirations, having tolerated the procedures well.

subperiosteal-maxilla-and-all-on-x-mandible:text:2 were present for the entirety of the procedure.
